BAHAMA ISLANDS
Area, etc.
The total land area of the Colony is 4,404 square miles. It is separated on the west from Florida by the Straits of Florida and on the south from Cuba by the Old Bahama and Nicholas Channels.
Population
The population at the end of 1964 was estimated at 138,149. English is universally spoken.
Principal Town
Nassau, the capital, is the chief port and the only town of any size. It is situated on the island of New Providence, the population of which is estimated to be 83,837.
Geographical Features

The Colony consists of an archipelago of about 700 islands, of which only 30 are inhabited, and over 2,000 rocks. The islands are generally long, narrow and low-lying and are of aeolian limestone formation. The soil, except in small pockets, is shallow and overlays hard rock. On the whole, it is suitable only for peasant farming. Conditions are sub-tropical. It is probable that at one time all the islands were thickly wooded but little forest now remains except on Abaco, Andros and Grand Bahama, where there are extensive pine forests.
Climate

The winter climate is delightful. Frost is unknown, the average temperature is 70°, the rainfall is slight and cool breezes prevail. In July, August and September the heat ranges from 80° to 90° and though the islands are never unhealthy, the change in temperature throughout the 24 hours is so small that those who remain in the Colony for the whole year may find the long summer somewhat enervating, though this is mitigated in modern days by air conditioning. Although the customarily recognised season when the majority of visitors come to the Bahamas is in the winter, there is a growing number of persons who prefer the summer months because of the opportunities for swimming, sailing and fishing.
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
The rainfall averages about 40 inches a year. The rainy months are May, June, September and October.
History

San Salvador, one of the islands composing the Colony, and identical with Watling's Island, was the first land discovered by Columbus on his voyage in 1492. A few years later all the Arawak inhabitants were transported to work in the Hispaniola mines. It does not appear that the Spaniards had settlements on any of the islands of the group at any time. In 1649 Parliament, on the petition of William Sayle, a former Governor of Bermuda, and others, passed "An Act for the Adventurers for the Eleutherian Islands" which constituted Sayle and his associates the proprietors of the islands. Notwithstanding an earlier Royal Grant in 1629 and the Cromwellian Act of 1649, Charles II in 1670 granted the islands to six of the Lords Proprietors of Carolina. Before the 1670 Grant the inhabitants of the islands had organized the settlement and instituted a form of government, which included an elective House of Assembly, and had selected their Governor. His appointment was later confirmed by the Lords Proprietors and a regular system of government was established including a parliament, the lower house of which was elective, and this was continued with several breaks until the civil and military government of the islands was resumed by the Crown in 1717.

The settlement on New Providence was sacked by the Spaniards on several occasions between 1680 and 1684. In 1684 all the inhabitants were driven away, and it was not until 1686 that the settlement was re-formed by their return, principally from Jamaica. By 1703 it had reached some importance, when it was almost annihilated by the French and Spaniards. However, a year or so after this the dispersed inhabitants returned once more. The islands became a regular rendezvous for pirates and this finally determined the Crown to resume the civil and military government of the place; since 1717 there has been a continuous line of Royal Governors. The islands were surrendered to a fleet of the American rebels in 1776, and again to the Spaniards in 1782, but were re-taken by a British force under Colonel Deveaux and British possession was confirmed by the Peace of Versailles, 1783. After the American Revolution a number of loyalists settled throughout the Bahamas group and received substantial assistance from England, and in 1787 the Lords Proprietors surrendered all their proprietary rights to the King for the sum of £12,000, which was provided by Parliament.

The constitution of the Bahamas was eventually settled in 1729, when the Crown assumed direct control of the colony. The government was modelled upon that of England in the early days, the Governor representing the Sovereign, and the nominated Legislative Council and the elected House of Assembly representing respectively the Houses of Lords and Commons.

Up to January, 1964, representative, but not responsible government existed. The executive government was in the hands of a Governor, appointed by the Crown; who had the power of veto, and was advised by an Executive Council of not more than nine members of whom six were unofficials. Various executive powers and the right to enact certain subsidiary legislation were vested by law in the Governor in Council. A
Legislative Council was created as a separate Council by Royal Letters Patent in 1841; and in 1963 it consisted of eleven members nominated by the Crown, of whom nine were unofficials.

The General Assembly Elections Act of 1959 provided for, inter alia , adult male suffrage, and a limited second vote in respect of ownership and/or rental of real property, the latter to be exercised only in a constituency other than that in which the elector resided. The Act also provided for the creation of two additional seats for the Southern and Eastern Districts of New Providence and the redistribution of the New Providence seats after by-elections to fill the new seats. Accordingly from 1960 the House of Assembly was composed of 33 members.
A law was promulgated with effect from mid-1962 extending the franchise to women and making women eligible for election to seats in the House of Assembly.
Constitution and Administration

At the Bahamas Constitutional Conference held in London in May, 1963, the Secretary of State agreed to further constitutional changes. These were embodied in a new Constitution which came into effect on the 7th January 1964, giving the territory full internal self-government.
The bicameral Legislature has been reconstituted to consist of an Upper House called the Senate and a Lower House called the House of Assembly.

The Senate consists of fifteen members of whom eight are appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier and such other persons as he may in his discretion decide to consult, five by the Governor on the advice of the Premier, and two by the Governor on the advice of the Leader of the Opposition.

The House of Assembly remains unchanged until it is dissolved in the ordinary course. Thereafter the House of Assembly will consist of thirty-eight elected members, of whom not less than eighteen nor more than twenty-two members will represent Out Island constituencies, and not less than sixteen nor more than twenty members will represent New Providence constituencies. The next General Election will be contested on the basis of twenty-one seats for the Out Islands and seventeen for New Providence.

The Cabinet consists of a Premier and fourteen Ministers. The Governor appoints as Premier the person who appears to him to be best able to command a majority in the House of Assembly. The remaining Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier.
Land Policy
The Status of Aliens Act Ch. 180 empowers an alien to acquire and hold property within the Colony.
Taxation

There is no income tax. Excise tax is charged on spirits and beer locally produced at the rate of four-fifths (4/5) of the import duty imposed on the particular kind of spirit or beer. The general Customs duty is 20% ad valorem plus 7½% ad valorem Emergency Tax. Staple foodstuffs pay a low specific duty and no Emergency Tax; spirits and tobacco are taxed at higher rates than the general and gasoline pays a specific duty with additional sales tax. Preferential margins are designed to implement the Ottawa and Anglo-American Trade Agreements.
Digitized by Google
BAHAMAS
Public Finance
Revenue Expenditure

The main sources of revenue are Customs dues and receipts from fees, Post Office, Licences and public utilities. In 1964 £6,875,588 of the revenue was derived from import duties. For the same year the main heads of expenditure were :
Contributions and Charitable Grants (£1,385,775), Ministry of Education (£1,163,647), Ministry of Health (£1,209,501), Ministry of Tourism (£1,233,656) and Ministry of Works (£907,169).
In December 1964 the outstanding amount of the authorized Public Debt was £2,426,151.
Currency

The legal tender currency is British sterling, although United States and Canadian currencies are also generally accepted in the Colony. Currency notes of the denominations of 4s., 10s., £1 and £5 are issued by the Bahamas Government. Accounts are kept in sterling. Notice has been given, however, of the intention to introduce decimal currency shortly. There are branch banks of the Royal Bank of Canada, Bank of Nova Scotia, Canadian Bank of Commerce, Barclays Bank, D.C. and O., Bank of London and Montreal, First National City Bank of New York, Wellington Bank of Canada, Ltd., and Chase Manhattan Bank in Nassau.
Development
Freeport is a booming holiday resort with diversified light industrial activity on Grand Bahama Island, third largest island in the Bahama Islands, 76 miles from Palm Beach and 120 miles from Miami.
The founder of Freeport is Wallace Groves, American lawyer-financier who formed the Grand Bahama Port Authority, Ltd., to negotiate the "Hawksbill Creek Agreement" with the Bahamas Government.

Among the Agreement's provisions is one stating that no real property taxes will be imposed until at least 1990; also that until that year, the Port Authority and its licensees will be free from Personal Property, Capital Gains and Capital Appreciation Taxes. The same applies to Authority and Licensee's earnings and the earnings and bonuses of their employees. The Agreement also provided that a harbour should be constructed before the Agreement could be ratified and an amendment to the Agreement had a condition that a luxury hotel should be completed by the end of 1963. The result of the vision behind the Agreement and the subsequent enterprise has been the unprecedented growth of a boom town in the sunshine.

A deep-water harbour enables Miami-Nassau cruise ships to make Freeport a Port of Call. The Freeport Bunkering Company, Ltd., refuels ships through underwater lines so that they are neither required to enter port nor pay port fees, taxes, duties or surcharges found at U.S. Bunkering Ports.
The largest single investment is the over $50 million plant of the Bahamas Cement Company,
Ltd., a wholly-owned subsidiary of U.S. Steel which began operations in December 1964.
The Freeport International Airport was completely modernized in 1964. A jet runway has been completed.

Adjacent to Freeport is the resort of Lucaya. The lavish new Lucayan Beach Hotel and Casino have nearby an 18-hole championship golf course designed by the late Dick Wilson and an ultra-modern marina and boatel. Sometimes referred to as the "New World Riviera", it is a tourist centre second only to Nassau.

Shipping magnate D. K. Ludwig is building a resort complex around his recently opened King's Inn and golf clubs. The Holiday Inn chain opened a 500-room beachfront hotel in the winter of 1965. Other hotels and apartments are in construction, as well as many homes.
Like all of the Bahamas, Freeport/Lucaya provides a benign climate and superb beaches.

A Master Plan for Freeport is in the making which will provide for a broad land utilization programme and a more detailed land utilization plan. The former will pinpoint locations for residential, industrial and commercial development while the latter will take into account streets and parking, transit and communications, schools and churches, parks and beaches and public buildings and public services.
Education

A new comprehensive Education Act known as the Education Act, 1962, and modelled in principle on the 1944 Education Act in the United Kingdom, came into effect on 1st September 1962. The new Act placed all government effort in the field of education under one authority, the Board of Education, and provided the machinery for the development of a modern system of education in the future.

The Act was amended in December 1963 in anticipation of the New Constitution which came into effect on 7th January 1964 and which gives the Bahamas internal self-government. All government effort in Education has now become the responsibility of the Minister for Education. The former Board of Education no longer exists but the Minister is advised by the Central Advisory Council for Education.
Under the Act, education is compulsory between the ages of 5 and 14. Of a total of 150 Government primary schools, 130 are Out Island all-age schools.
There is one reformatory school for boys under the Industrial School Act and one for girls.
No local provision is made for Higher Education and students seeking this type of training must go abroad. Bahamians studying abroad in 1964/65 session were:
135 in the United Kingdom
100 approximately in the United States of America
12 in Canada (as far as can be ascertained)
9 in University of the West Indies
Total 256.
A special relationship has been established with the University of the West Indies and it is anticipated that many more students will attend this University in the future.
The Teachers' Training College which was temporarily closed in 1957 was re-opened in September 1961.

The Minister for Education is responsible for initiating policy on scholarships and for awarding all scholarships. He is advised on the actual number and type of scholarships to be awarded by an Advisory Council, and the proposals finalized by the Minister are approved by the Cabinet. All scholarships are advertised and all applications considered by a Selection Committee, which advises the Minister on the awards. The final selection is in the hands of the Minister.
In service training is the responsibility of each department; but their proposals must be approved by the Public Service Commission.

Libraries
There are three Public Libraries in Nassau and 31 in the Out Islands.
Health
The Curative needs of the Colony are met by:

(a) Princess Margaret Hospital with disciplines and full-time Consultant Specialists in Medicine, Surgery, Anaesthesia, Paediatrics, Chest Diseases, Pathology, Radiology, Obstetrics & Gynaecology and Ophthalmology. An O.P.D. with 24-hour Emergency Casualty Service is provided.
(b) The Mental Hospital with Consultant Psychiatrist.
(c) The Geriatric Hospital.
(d) Out Islands District Medical Officers (6).
(e) Out Islands Clinics (42).

There is also a Health Centre and a Cottage Hospital in Eleuthera and a further Cottage Hospital in Inagua. A District Nursing Service exists. Public Health is administered from Nassau and the Public Health Nursing Service and Schools Medical and Dental Service cater for all preventive services.
In Nassau there is the Private Rassin Clinic.
Medical care is free to the needy and sub-economic rates are charged others.

In 1964, there were 12,630 admissions and 461 deaths. Maternity cases were 3,950. In the Casualty O.P.D., 34,185 patients were seen. In general the pattern of diseases is subtropical and not tropical, with respiratory diseases of all ages present and Tuberculosis appearing to be capable of control, hypertensive states common and social diseases e.g., Amoebiasis, Enteritis, Venereal diseases and malnutrition all associated with poor housing and utilities common in those areas concerned.

The health of the general public remains satisfactory and the Public Health Nursing Service increased its numbers in Infant Welfare, Immunisation and Vaccination, Home visiting, Ante and post-natal, and Communicable diseases services.

Communications

Nassau International Airport is the main airfield in the Bahamas. It provides 24 hours service for long range international and local airlines. In addition, there are 36 landing strips completed in the various Out Islands. Scheduled international air services were operated into Nassau by B.O.A.C. from London via New York and Bermuda and from Montego Bay, Jamaica; Pan American World Airways operate daily flights from Miami and New York; Trans Canada Airlines from Montreal via Tampa, Florida and from Jamaica; Mackey Airlines from Tampa, Fort Lauderdale, Palm Beach, Miami and Jacksonville; Bahamas Airways Limited operates between Nassau, Fort Lauderdale and Miami several times daily; Quantas operate once weekly in each direction between London, Nassau and Sydney, Australia.

Bahamas Airways Limited operates daily scheduled flights to many of the Out Islands. Bahamas Airways Limited and U.S.A.-Bahamas Air Services operate charter flights using land-planes between the Out Islands and between the Colony and Florida. There are 22 charter operators based in Florida licensed to provide charter services between Florida and the Bahamas.
The Freeport International Airport on Grand Bahama Island was completely modernised in 1964. It now has a new international runway of 8,300 feet capable of taking jet airliners.
During 1964, 19,500 landings were recorded at Nassau International Airport.

On Eleuthera there is an asphalt main road some 100 miles in length covering the whole of the Island and on Grand Bahama, 65 miles of motor road has been built. Asphalt roads are under construction at Andros, Exuma, Eleuthera, Cat Island, Spanish Wells, San Salvador, Long Island and Crooked Island, where previously only rough tracks existed.
There are no railways in the Colony.

There are regular freight services between the Colony and New York and Florida; Halifax and Montreal (Canadian National Steamship Line); and the United Kingdom (Royal Mail Line and Pacific Steam Navigation Company). West India Steamships Ltd. operate a service

between Nassau and Inagua with intermediate stops.
In 1964 7,577 ships entered and 4,867 were cleared at Nassau. Their total tonnage was 11,025,877, of which 1,838,671 tons was British.
Ships also call at Inagua and Pine Ridge to take on salt and lumber exports.
Sound and Television Broadcasting

Government owns, and the Bahamas Broadcasting and Television Commission operates, a broadcasting station located in Nassau. The power of its transmitter is 10 kW., frequency 1,540 kc/s and call sign ZNS. Commercial operation began in 1950, though a broadcasting station has existed since 1936. The Commission took over on 1st January 1957. The broadcast receiver licence fee was abolished in 1954. At that time there were 5,990 licence holders. Government supplied and maintains 60 community receivers in school-houses in the Out Islands where there may be as many as 200 listeners in the evening.

In August 1962, a second channel opened transmission on a frequency of 1,240 kc/s with a power of 250 watts. The call sign is ZNS II. This covers the island of New Providence only. Commercial advertisements are restricted to single spots between programmes.
Principal Occupations
The tourist traffic continues to be the main industry of the Colony and is the chief source of revenue and of dollars.
The following table shows the number and nationality of visitors to New Providence during the years 1958-64:
Nationality—per cent.
Agricultural work in the United States provides employment for an average of 642 unskilled Bahamian labourers and their earnings in 1964 amounted to £633,795.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are fresh vegetables, tomatoes, okra, pineapples, peas, citrus, bananas, avocados, mangos and sisal. Some sugar cane is grown in the Colony.

The Minister for Agriculture and Fisheries is fully alive to the importance of expanding the livestock husbandry of the Colony and of improving the quality of the products. This is being attained by the importation of purebred animals to facilitate the up-grading of local stocks of sheep and pigs; by the provision of pastures for small groups of farmers and by encouraging the establishment of herds of beef and dairy cattle. The estimated livestock population is: sheep 22,800, goats 14,000, swine 10,700, cattle 3,500, horses 3,550, poultry 620,000.
Crawfishing and scale fishing are extensively carried out during the open season (September 1st to March 15th). The bulk of the catch is exported to the United States. The following
marine products are also exported: sponge, salt, turtle shell, beach shells and marine curios

There are four canning plants in the Colony which are engaged principally in canning tomatoes and pineapples. Straw products are manufactured as cottage industries and the raw material for this work is obtained chiefly from palm frond and sisal fibre. Most of the canneries' output is consumed locally.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The Bahamas Produce Exchange in Nassau handles much of the agricultural produce of the Colony. It assists the farmers in New Providence and Out Islands in disposing of their produce without glutting the market and depressing prices unnecessarily.

There are no co-operative societies in the Colony other than 17 Tomato Growers' Associations on the Islands of Eleuthera and Cat Island and 21 active Farmers' Associations situated throughout the Colony. These associations are assisted by the Ministry of Agriculture and Fisheries to the extent of advancing seed and fertilizer, etc., for the tomato and other crops. The Ministry markets the crops and deducts the advances from the proceeds of each consignor.

BARBADOS
Area, etc.
Barbados is the most easterly of the Caribbean islands. Its total area is 166 square miles.
Population
On the 31st December 1964, the estimated population was 244,169.
Principal Town
Bridgetown, the capital, is the only town. Before the 1960 census its population was estimated at about 11,304.
Geographical Features

The Island is comparatively flat, rising in a series of tablelands marked by well-defined terraces to the highest point (1,104 feet) at Mount Hillaby. The north-east corner of the Island, the Scotland area, is broken country, much eroded and rather barren. The formation of the rest of the Island is coral limestone. There are no rivers, but deep gullies which fill with water during heavy rain have cut their way through the coral terraces in many places. Indigenous forest covers about 46 acres.
Climate

The climate is extremely pleasant. North-easterly trade winds blow steadily from December to June but during the remainder of the year, the wet season, the wind moves to the south-east and is less strong, resulting in humid, hotter conditions. The average temperature is $79.8^{\circ}$ . The rainfall is very varied: in the high central district the yearly average is 75 inches while in some of the low-lying coastal areas the average is 50 inches.
History

Uncertainty remains on some points in the early history of Barbados and differing versions have appeared from time to time. The brief account which follows is based on what is taken to be the most authentic version.

As far as is known the original inhabitants were the Arawak Indians who migrated from the mainland of South America. When they arrived is not known, but relics of their occupation down to the latter years of the sixteenth century have been discovered in various parts of the Island. By the time the English settlers came they had disappeared, partly through Carib raids from the neighbouring islands, and partly through the Spaniards who deported them to work in the mines of Hispaniola.

In spite of the belief to the contrary, there is no satisfactory evidence that the Olive Branch under Captain Catlin ever touched at Barbados when it was driven off its course from England to the River Wiapoco settlement in 1605 and fetched up at
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

t. Lucia. The first Englishman to visit Barbados may have been Captain Simon Gordan, who claimed that he had landed some time before the settlement of 1627 and had found the Island uninhabited. He may, however, have been on a ship under the command of Sir Thomas Warner that visited sometime between 1620 and 1624 and found the Island suffering from such a severe drought that the latter preferred settlement of his colonists at St. Kitts. In 1624 or early 1625 a ship, belonging to Sir William Courteen and under the command of Captain John Powell, touched at Barbados, when en route from Pernambuco to England; some men were landed who erected a cross and on a tree nearby inscribed "James K. of E. and of this Island". On receiving Powell's information about Barbados Sir William Courteen fitted out an expedition to the Island under the command of his informant; but Powell, who carried letters of marque, captured a Spanish or Portuguese prize and returned with it to Cowes in 1626. Courteen then fitted out a second expedition and in February 1627, the William and John under the command of Captain Henry Powell—Captain John Powell's younger brother—landed 80 settlers. During 1627 and 1628 the colony grew rapidly and by the end of 1628 it was claimed that Courteen had planted 1,850 settlers. The Earl of Carlisle obtained a grant from Charles I for all the Caribbean islands and landed settlers in 1628. After his death his son leased the Island in 1647 for 21 years to Lord Willoughby of Parham who assumed the government of the Island and during the Civil War held it for the Royalists until his capitulation to the Cromwellian fleet in 1652. A Governor was appointed by the Commonwealth in place of Lord Willoughby and at the Restoration the claims of the Carlisle Patent were revived. An agreement was reached by which the Patent was surrendered to the Crown for compensation in the form of $\frac{1}{2}\%$ duty on exports. This duty was a continual source of complaint but was not abolished until 1838 by Act of Imperial Parliament. The importance of the agreement, however, is that it marked the end of proprietary rule. Since its settlement Barbados has been a possession of Great Britain.
Constitution

Barbados has one of the oldest constitutions in the Commonwealth and one in which conventions play an important part. Amendments to the constitution, providing for full internal self-government came into operation on the 16th October 1961, and further amendments were made and embodied in Statutory Instrument No. 491 which was brought into operation on 9th April 1964.

The Legislature consists of the Governor, an Upper House—the Senate—consisting of 21 members appointed by the Governor, twelve of whom are appointed on the advice of the Premier and two on the advice of the Leader of the Opposition; and a Lower House—the House of Assembly—consisting of 24 members elected by universal adult suffrage. The life of both Houses is now limited to five years.

The revised Constitutional Instruments include provision for a Privy Council, a Cabinet, and the establishment of Judicial and Legal Service, Public Service and Police Service Commissions with executive powers to deal with appointments, dismissals and disciplinary control of members of the services for which they are responsible.
The Privy Council consists of the Governor and other persons (at present four, including the

Minister designated Attorney-General and the Premier) appointed by Her Majesty. It advises the Governor in the exercise of the Royal Prerogative of Mercy and in the exercise of his disciplinary powers over members of the Public Service and the Police Service who may appeal to the Privy Council against disciplinary action recommended to be taken against them.
The Cabinet consists of the Premier and not less than 5 other Ministers—there are at present 7—appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier; it is the principal instrument of policy.

The Governor appoints as Premier the member of the House of Assembly who appears to him best able to command the support of the majority of the members of the Assembly. On the Premier's advice the Governor appoints other Ministers from among the Senators and the members of the House of Assembly. The Premier and other Ministers hold office during the Governor's pleasure.
Land Policy
The systems of land tenure are similar to those of the United Kingdom.
Taxation

Income tax is the main form of direct taxation. The tax rates range from 3 cents on every complete $ of taxable income up to $500, to 75 cents on every complete $ of taxable income beyond $24,000. Tax is not paid by residents on incomes below $800 per annum in the case of single individuals and $1,400 in the case of married persons. A wife's earned income allowance not exceeding $300 is granted. Deductions from assessable income of $270 to $600 (varying according to the age of the child, and educational establishment at which full time instruction is being received) are allowed for children and $240 for other dependants, in the latter case not exceeding two. Deductions are also permitted in respect of life assurance premiums paid. Medical expenses may be deducted in excess of $20 but not exceeding in the aggregate $100, in the case of a tax-payer of married status, or $50 in the case of any other person, plus $25 for each child, not exceeding two. Companies pay a flat rate of 40%. The rate for life assurance companies and building societies is the average paid by an individual with a taxable income of $4,800.
A non-cumulative system of Pay As You Earn is in operation.

Estate duties range from 1% on estates exceeding $4,800 and not exceeding $14,400 to 30% on estates over $1,920,000. In addition, succession duty ranges from $\frac{1}{2}\%$ between $96 and $2,400 to 2% of values exceeding $9,600 where the successor is a lineal descendant or ancestor 1% to 6% where successor is a brother, sister or their descendants, and from 2% to 10% where the successor is a stranger.
Entertainment tax is charged on certain forms of entertainment.
The other main forms of taxation are the Customs tariffs and excise duty on rum production.

Development Plans
The Legislature has approved a Development Plan for 1965-68 envisaging a total expenditure of $41.4 million as follows:—

($ million)

Education 4.6
Health and Housing 5.9
Communications and Works 14.7
Development, Industry and Tourism 9.4

41.4

($ million)
Surplus Balances 15.1
Grants 3.4
Revenue of Statutory Bodies 2.1
Sugar Reserve Funds 3.8
Loan Funds 17.0
41.4
The ultimate objective of the plan is to secure higher living standards, an equitable distribution of the increased disposable income and a happier and full life for the whole society.
To achieve this the plan sets out the following immediate goals:
(1) To accelerate the rate of growth of per capital real national income by increasing output especially in the Agricultural and manufacturing sectors and net earnings of the tourist industry.
(2) To stimulate exports.
(3) To maintain stable prices within the limits set by world market conditions.
(4) To intensify its population control programme.
(5) To improve and expand the health, housing and educational facilities of the island.
It is anticipated that in the period of the plan the gross domestic product will rise by 4% annually from $143 million in 1965 to $168 million in 1968.

So far as Agriculture is concerned the background to the plan is the assumption that sugar production will remain at a relatively stable level for the foreseeable future and so the main emphasis in increasing agricultural output is on the non-sugar section, principally livestock, root crops, vegetable production, dairy products and fisheries. To achieve this, the plan provides for various incentive schemes such as, payments for livestock keepers and for the encouragement of pangola grass production, promotion of co-operatives, continued use of the Barbados Marketing Corporation as the Government's chief instrument for stimulating local production of food crops, fish and meat, the establishment of an Agricultural Development Corporation responsible for the operation of all government owned estates and agricultural projects on a commercial basis, and the continuance and intensification of research projects aimed at raising the overall efficiency of the agricultural sector.

With regard to industrial development the main objective is to raise the contribution of manufacturing to the Gross Domestic Product. Policy is aimed at overcoming the handicaps of a small domestic market and lack of indigenous resources by the offer of special inducements. It is proposed to abolish the Development Board and replace it with an Industrial Development Corporation which will take over the promotional functions of the Board, and a Development Bank which will take over the Loan making functions of the Board. Overseas offices for promoting the island as an industrial centre will be set up in London, Toronto and New York. Factory space in developed industrial estates will be provided, loans will be available for prospective investors, and certain additional fiscal incentives will be introduced.

To further encourage the expanding tourist industry it is proposed to expand recreational facilities, to establish a free port shopping area within the Bridgetown Harbour, to continue and expand the training facilities for hotel workers and carry out a classification of hotels.

The plan provides for improvement of the existing system of internal and external communications by continuance of the road rehabilitation and building programmes, extension and renovation of Seawell Airport and the undertaking of technical and economic feasibility studies into the proposal to deepen and extend berthing facilities at the Bridgetown Harbour.

In the social field the plan provides for expansion of educational facilities at the primary and secondary levels, the overhauling and expansion of technical education, the establishment of district hospitals, the expansion of environmental sanitation schemes, the establishment of children's homes, expansion of recreational facilities for all sections of the community, launching of a Social Security Scheme, continuance of the housing and slum clearance programme, and the establishment of a Land Development Corporation to administer a scheme for integrated housing development through mortgage insurance.
Education

Education is provided by Government at the following levels: Primary, Secondary, University, Adult and Further. Primary Education is provided in Government Primary Schools, Secondary in the Senior Departments of the Primary Schools, the Government Comprehensive (formerly Secondary Modern) Schools, the Secondary Grammar Schools maintained by Government and in some
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Private Independent Schools. Present policy aims at the introduction of compulsory education for all children between the ages of 5 and 14 years.

Tuition at the Primary and Comprehensive levels is free. Tuition fees were abolished for Barbadian children in the Government maintained Grammar Schools from January 1962. The Preparatory Departments of these schools were abolished at the same time. A Common Entrance Examination for children between the ages of 10 and 12 was instituted for entry to Government Grammar Schools. Improved and additional accommodation in the Primary and Comprehensive Schools is being provided under a School Building Programme by the extension and replacement of existing schools and construction of new schools where necessary.

There were, in 1964, 117 Primary Schools (31 for boys, 34 for girls and 52 co-educational, including 6 infant, schools), 5 Comprehensive Schools and 10 Secondary Grammar Schools entirely maintained by Government. The number of children in the Primary Schools at the end of the year 1963-64 was 42,700 (21,658 boys, 21,042 girls), in Comprehensive Schools 5,584 (2,902 boys, 2,682 girls), in Secondary Grammar Schools 4,434 (2,609 boys and 1,825 girls). There were 117 Headteachers and 811 Assistants in the Primary Schools, 5 Headteachers and 190 Assistants in the Comprehensive Schools, 10 Headteachers and 195 Assistants in the Grammar Schools and 160 Supernumerary and 326 Relief Teachers in the Primary and Comprehensive Schools respectively.

Erdiston Training College: with an intake of 120 students offers simultaneously one-year and two-year training courses for teachers including 30 teachers from the Leeward and Windward Islands. Teacher-training is also provided by means of Induction Classes and vacation inservice courses.

Technical and Vocational Education is provided: at the Industrial Arts Wings of Comprehensive and Grammar Schools for boys, at the Barbados Technical Institute (enrolment 553) which offers both day and evening classes from the pre-apprenticeship level to the standard of craftsmen and City and Guilds Institute of London certificate, and through a system of Apprenticeship Bursaries for school-leavers financed by Government. The Housecraft Centre specialises in Domestic Science and Home Economics training for girls.

Government awards from its own resources yearly: five Barbados Scholarships (one restricted to girls) to the University of the West Indies and Commonwealth Universities overseas, two exhibitions, three special awards (in Engineering, Agriculture and one for girls) and ten enabling bursaries all to the University of the West Indies, as well as teachers scholarships and enabling grants to students of exhibition standard for study at University level. There is also a Higher Education Loan Fund, established in 1953, from which loans are made to students for University study abroad. A number of awards, Commonwealth scholarships for example, are also available from donor Governments and external agencies annually for University and postgraduate study abroad.

Government makes an annual subsidy towards the University of the West Indies which now has constituent colleges in Jamaica, Trinidad and Barbados. In addition Government meets the tuition fees of all Barbadians who are full-time students of the local Colleges of Arts and Science established in 1963. Codrington College, formerly
a degree-granting affiliate of Durham University in England, is now a theological seminary.

During the academic year 1963-64 there were 97 Government scholars, exhibitioners, teacher trainees and bursars attending universities overseas—37 in the United Kingdom, 2 in Canada and 58 at the University of the West Indies. 35 Students were granted loans from the Higher Education Loan Fund. Of these 10 were attending universities in the United Kingdom, 17 were at the University of the West Indies, 1 at a university in the United States of America, 3 in Canada, 3 in India and 1 in Germany. In addition, there were some 20 students taking advantage of awards from external aid agencies and donor Governments for further studies abroad.

Evening classes in academic, commercial and technical subjects and in the fields of Handicraft and Home Economics are conducted under the aegis of the Barbados Evening Institute and through the Extra Mural Department of the University of the West Indies at some 27 centres throughout the island. Students enrolled in the Institute's classes during 1962-63 numbered 2,702.
Expenditure on education in the financial year April 1963—March 1964 was $5,895,634 compared with $1,974,705 in 1952-53 and $566,205 in 1942-43.
Libraries and Museums

A free Public Library (100,847 books) is maintained by Government. The Barbados Museum and Historical Society possesses collections of books, pictures, models, specimens of birds and fishes, and a collection of relics of the original Arawak inhabitants of the island.
Health

The Queen Elizabeth Hospital which has accommodation for 546 non-paying and 87 paying patients was officially opened by H.R.H. The Duke of Edinburgh on 14th November 1964. This Hospital replaces the Old Barbados General Hospital. Between 1st January and 31st December 1964, hospital admissions numbered 11,094 and out patient attendances 136,206. The Government Maternity Hospital has been incorporated in and now forms part of the Queen Elizabeth Hospital. The Government maintains a Mental Hospital with accommodation for 321 males and 441 females, and a Leper Hospital at which there are 12 patients. For the training of midwives the Government Maternity Hospital has accommodation for 20 expectant mothers. Each of the 11 parishes has an infirmary for the care of the destitute sick and for the housing of the aged and poor. These infirmaries provide medical care and in some parishes function as district hospitals. The District Hospital at Oistins, Christ Church, provides 20 beds: 10 for maternity and 10 for ante-natal cases.

Preventive medicine is based on the Health Centre system. Three centres are operating in St. Michael, St. Peter and St. Philip. Two of these centres operate out-station clinics in various parts of the Island.
Estimated Central Government expenditure of Health Services amounted to $3,975,405 in 1964-65 compared with $206,870 in 1935-36.
Communications
An International Airport is situated at Seawell, 12 miles from Bridgetown. Air France, British Overseas Airways Corporation, British West
Digitized by Google
BARBADOS
Indian Airways, Leeward Island Air Transport, Pan American World Airways and Trans-Canada Air Lines operate frequent scheduled services connecting Barbados with the major world air routes.
There are 800 miles of roads open of which approximately 720 miles have an asphalt finish.
Bridgetown is the only port of entry, although some loading of sugar takes place at Speightstown and oil is pumped ashore at Spring Gardens and an Esso installation on the West Coast.

The main shipping companies visiting Barbados are Harrison Line, Geest Line, Royal Netherlands Steamship Company, Compagnie Générale Transatlantique, Saguenay Shipping Ltd., Booth Line, Lamport and Holt Line, Alcoa Steamship Company, M.A.N.Z. Line, Moore McCormack Line, Nourse Line, Caribbean-Hamburg Line (formerly Three Bays Line) and Federal Shipping Service. Occasionally ships of the Linea "C" and Grimaldi Lines visit port.
Broadcasting and Television

Barbados has a television service, a wireless broadcasting service and a wired broadcasting service. The first two are operated by the Caribbean Broadcasting Corporation, a corporate body set up by Order-in-Council of the Barbados Government in 1963. The wired system, which covers the whole island and had 25,788 subscribers at the 31st December 1964, is operated by Barbados Rediffusion Service Limited, a local subsidiary of the London firm of Rediffusion Limited.
Principal Occupations

Most of the population is engaged in the cultivation of the sugar crop and auxiliary occupations connected with the sugar industry. Approximate figures are: 18,000 persons engaged in agriculture, 2,000 in domestic service, 12,500 in the retail trade and 8,500 in construction work. About 1,650 persons are engaged in the fishing industry.
At the end of 1963 there were nine Trade Unions of which 3 were employers' associations with membership totalling about 340 business concerns and six workers' organizations with about 15,500 members.
Main Crops and Products
Sugar cane is the principal export crop grown on all plantations and on nearly all small holdings. In 1965 sugar production was the equivalent of 195,973 long tons.

Yams, sweet potatoes, eddoes, cassava, maize and pigeon peas comprise the main food crops which are grown in accordance with regulations issued annually. Green vegetables and other crops are cultivated on a minor scale and there is a limited production of tree crops and citrus fruit. A marketing Corporation has been established by statute for the purpose of improving the facilities for marketing in order to encourage an increase in the production of food crops, meat and dairy produce and fish.
A Central Livestock Station has been established to encourage the development of the local livestock industry including dairying as a major agricultural industry.

A programme of soil conservation control and rehabilitation work is being carried out in the Scotland District which is an area comprising one-seventh of the island's acreage with very intractable soils on steeply sloping land, with a view to increasing the productivity of the area.

The fishing fleet has been completely mechanised with the assistance of loans from the Government and Cold storage facilities provided by the Marketing Corporation. A shrimp industry has been developed. The value of fish and shrimp caught during 1964 was estimated at W.I. $1.15 million and W.I. $2.48 million respectively.
Production of natural gas during the year ended 31st March 1965 was 97,122 million cubic feet. During 1964 138,690 tons of temper, building and manurial limestone were exported.

Manufactures include the production of sugar for local consumption, rum, bread and a variety of biscuits, edible oil, aerated waters, cigarettes, soap, ice, furniture, jewellery, mattresses, lumber, ready-to-wear garments, shoes, batteries and building products.

BASUTOLAND
Area

Comprises an area of 11,716 square miles of which about one quarter in the west is lowland country varying in height above sea level from 5,000 to 6,000 feet, the remaining three quarters being highlands rising to a height of 11,425 feet in the Drakensberg Range, which forms the eastern boundary with Natal.
Population
In 1956 (the date of the last census) the population figures for Maseru township were: Africans 5,091, Europeans 590, Mixed Race 57 and one Asian.

A census of all races held in April 1956 gave the following figures for the population present in the Territory: Africans 638,857; Europeans 1,926; Persons of Mixed Race 644; and Asians 247. The census also gave the additional figures of 154,782 as an estimate of the number of Africans temporarily absent from the Colony for work and other purposes. The total population of Basutoland, including those temporarily absent, is estimated at 884,342 persons in 1965. (By way of comparison, a census of 1875 gave a total of 469 Europeans and 127,707 Africans in Basutoland.)
Principal Towns
The capital is Maseru, which has an estimated population of about 10,000. The other principal towns are: Butha Buthe, Leribe, Mafeteng, Mokhotlong, Qachas Nek, Mohale's Hoek and Quthing.
Geographical Features

The Colony of Basutoland is an enclave within the Republic of South Africa, its boundaries running with those of Natal to the east, the Cape Province to the south and the Orange Free State to the north and west. It lies between latitudes $28^{\circ} 35'$ and $30^{\circ} 40'$ south and longitudes $27^{\circ} 00'$ and $29^{\circ} 30'$ east. The mountain ranges run from north to south and those in the central area, named the Maluti, are spurs of the main Drakensberg which they join in the north, forming a high plateau varying in height from 9,000 to 10,500 feet. In this area two of the largest rivers in the Republic, the Orange and the Tugela, and tributaries of the Caledon, have their source.
Climate
Rainfall is variable and averages approximately 29 inches a year over the greater part of the country. Most of it falls between October and April, but
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

there is normally no month which has less than half an inch. Unfortunately, river discharge statistics show that most of this water is lost to Basutoland in the form of run-off. The deciding factors from an agricultural point of view are whether the rain comes in steady soaking showers at intervals suited to the growth of the staple crops or whether it comes in the form of short and heavy storms, running to waste and eroding the soil. Serious droughts, like the one of 1948-49 are rare and there are a large number of perennial streams, though not so many as existed some years ago. In the lowlands the temperatures vary from a maximum of $90^\circ$ or more in summer to a minimum that rarely drops below $20^\circ$ in winter: in the highlands the range is much wider and temperatures below zero are common.
History

The Basotho as a nation did not come into prominence until 1818 when Moshesh, a minor chief of the Bakoena tribe in Northern Basutoland, gathered together the remnants of the various clans that had been scattered by the raids of the Zulu and Matebele.

The new nation went through a period of struggle, first with the emigrant Boers of the Great Trek, then with the British. From 1856 until 1868 there was a series of wars with the Orange Free State burghers, Moshoeshoe lost a large portion of his country and repeatedly sought the protection of the British Government.
In 1868 the Basotho became British subjects and the boundaries of the Territory were defined by the Cape of Good Hope Notice of 13th May 1870.

The country remained in a very unsettled condition until it was annexed to the Cape Colony in 1871. The people never took kindly to the Cape Colonial rule and in 1880, after various disturbances, the Governor of the Cape extended to Basutoland the provisions of the Cape Peace Preservation Act of 1878, which included a clause for the general disarmament of the Basotho. They refused to accept the terms, and, after a war lasting nearly a year, the Act was repealed, several chiefs would not submit, and it was decided to hand over the administration to the Imperial Government. This was effected by Order in Council of 2nd February 1884.

The South Africa Act of Union of 1909, which established the Union of South Africa, included provisions for the possible inclusion in South Africa of the three territories of Basutoland, Bechuanaland and Swaziland, which were administered by the High Commissioner for South Africa.

When the South African Constitution was being drawn up the Chiefs in Basutoland, Bechuanaland and Swaziland objected to any scheme which would bring their territories under the rule of South Africa. Assurances were given that no immediate change would be made in the administration of these territories, but provision was made for the possible eventual transfer subject to certain conditions for the protection of African rights embodied in the Act.

After 1909, successive South African Governments asked for the three territories to be transferred to South Africa and successive British Governments reiterated the pledge made during the passage of the 1909 South Africa Bill through Parliament, that the ultimate responsibility in the questions of a decision about transfer rested with the British Government and that no such transfer
could take place until the wishes of the inhabitants of the territories had been considered. No requests for transfer have been made recently.
Constitution

Before the introduction of constitutional reforms in March 1960 Basutoland was governed under a loose-knit system by which the British Administration combined with an hereditary Chieftainship. The Basotho owe allegiance to a single Paramount Chief under whom there is a hierarchy of Chiefs and Headmen. These Chiefs and Headmen are the authorities with whom the ordinary people deal in their daily affairs, and are responsible for the day to day administration of the Territory.

The constitution which was brought into operation in March 1960 provided for an Executive Council which is advisory to both the Resident Commissioner and the Paramount Chief, and a Legislative Council known as the Basutoland National Council.

In September 1961 the Basutoland National Council passed a resolution inviting the Paramount Chief to appoint a Constitutional Commission to review the working of the Constitution and to formulate proposals for constitutional advance.

The Constitutional Commission, which was appointed early in 1962 and which consisted of representatives of the main political parties and of the Chieftainship, submitted a report to the Paramount Chief on 3rd October 1963.

In May 1964 there was a further Constitutional Conference at which agreement was reached on the introduction of a new constitution which would be followed by independence if the desire for it was confirmed by the people of Basutoland through their Parliament or through a referendum not earlier than one year after election. The Basutoland Order 1965 embodying the new constitution, was brought into operation on 30th April 1965, the date following the elections for the new National Assembly.
The result of the election was:—
Basutoland National Party .. 31 seats
Basutoland Congress Party .. 25 seats
Marema-Tlou Freedom Party .. 4 seats

Under the new constitution, there are two Houses of Parliament, a Senate consisting of the 22 principal chiefs and 11 other persons nominated by the Paramount Chief, and a National Assembly consisting of 60 members elected on universal adult suffrage. The Office of Resident Commissioner ceased to exist and the Paramount Chief became the Queen's Representative. A new Office of British Government Representative was created.

At talks held in London during November, 1965 agreement was reached for the British Government Representative to assign the greater part of his responsibilities for the subjects of external affairs, internal security and defence to Basutoland Ministers with effect from the 1st January, 1966, subject to conditions which are listed in the Report of the discussions. The Public Service Commission and the Judicial Service Commission become executive on the same date. The British Government Representative retains reserved powers to ensure proper financial administration.
Taxation
The main forms of direct taxation are Basuto tax, income tax and poll tax (payable by all male adult persons who do not pay Basuto tax).

BASUTOLAND

The principal sources of indirect revenue are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Basutoland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades and businesses. There are also death duties, stamp duty and a wool and mohair export duty.

As revenue was expected to be substantially below expenditure and the Territory had no central balance the United Kingdom Government approved grant-in-aid of £2,350,000 for 1965-66.
Currency

The currency is the same as in the Republic of South Africa. During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from Sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 Sterling = R.2; 1 shilling = 10 cents).
Development Plans
Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act 1959 Basutoland received an allocation of £1.2m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959-64.
An additional allocation of £418,000 was made in 1961.

A further allocation of £1.2m. was approved in September 1963, to provide for development up to March 1966. The programme provides principally for agricultural, medical, educational and public works services.
Education

African education is largely in the hands of three missions (Paris Evangelical, Roman Catholic and Anglican), under the direction of the Education Department. Grants-in-aid are paid by Government to the three missions to cover the salaries of all teachers on the approved grant-in-aid list. In many aided schools of the Roman Catholic mission, assistance is given by unaided teachers, particularly Sisters. Small grants are paid from territorial funds to improve the equipment in primary schools. Estimated expenditure on grants-in-aid during financial year 1964-65 is £618,325.

There are 1,095 schools and institutions in the Territory of which all but 13 are under mission control, and of these mission schools 980 are either wholly or partly aided. The total enrolment in primary schools is 165,041, with 2,681 teachers of whom 1,059 have no professional certificates. In secondary schools the enrolment is 2,697, with 126 teachers all with professional training. The enrolment in the teacher training schools is 574, in schools offering a technical course and in housecraft schools 645.

The University of Basutoland, Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland formed by Royal Charter, formally came into existence on the 1st January 1964, and is the independent successor to the Roman Catholic Pius XII College at Roma. It awards its own degrees, and is intended by 1967 to have some 300 students primarily, but not exclusively, from Basutoland, Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland. Students are to be enrolled regardless of race, sex, nationality or religion. The immediate aim of the University is to meet the needs of Basutoland, Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland for qualified administrators in the Civil Service and the Judiciary, and in the trading profession, and to produce graduates qualified in Agriculture, Law, Economics and Commerce.
Tuition in primary schools is free, and bursaries are available for the most deserving cases at all stages of secondary, teacher training and University work.
The number of known Basotho students abroad on full time courses of a year's duration or more at the end of 1964 was 119, an increase of some 32 on the figure for the end of 1963.
Health

The establishment of the Government medical staff consists of a Permanent Secretary, a Senior Medical Officer, one Surgeon Specialist, 21 Medical Officers, one Medical Officer of Health, one Medical Officer for Mental Health. There is also one post of Dental Surgeon. There are nine Government hospitals, staffed by European Nursing Sisters with subordinate African staff. There are nine Government Hospitals, all of which, except two, are staffed by African Nursing Sisters with subordinate African staff. There is accommodation for 822 patients in the Government Hospitals. The Queen Elizabeth II Hospital in Maseru, is being extended by two wards, one surgical and the other for children, and when these are complete there will be accommodation for about 310 beds and a modern Nurses' Home.

The Mission Hospitals have increased to eight, (all Government subsidised) with the opening of Tebellong and 'Ma-Mohau hospitals, both in the mountain areas. They provide 537 beds. The overall bed-population ratio is about 1 to 600, whilst the Doctor-population ratio is about 1 to 33,000. Health centres in mountain dispensaries provide out-patient medical facilities, and, where a trained African nurse is a member of the staff, maternity services to people living in remote areas. During 1964 19,345 patients were admitted to Government hospitals and 7,097 to Mission hospitals. There were 236,994 out-patients at Government hospitals and 67,738 at Mission hospitals. The population of the Leper Settlement, four miles out of Maseru remains relatively static. The new Mental Hospital in the Leper Settlement grounds is nearing completion and will provide accommodation for up to 120 patients.

The major health problems are associated with deficiency diseases, attributable to poor nutrition education and poor agricultural methods. The incidence of tuberculosis is understandably also high. Lack of vital statistics, e.g. births and deaths, makes it impossible to give accurate data. Diseases due to poor environmental conditions e.g. enteric fever are endemic, and the infectious diseases take a heavy toll of infants and children.

Communications

The Territory is linked with the railway system of the Republic of South Africa by a short branch-line from Maseru, the capital, to Marseilles on the Bloemfontein-Natal main line. One mile of this line is in Basutoland. The South African Railway runs near the boundaries of the Territory and goods are transported by road to the nearest railway station across the border.

The Road Motor Transport Department of the South African Railways runs regular services between Fouriesburg and Butha-Buthe, Zastron and Quthing, Zastron and Mohale's Hoek, and Quthing and Sebapala, and within the Territory in the lowland areas there are a number of bus services owned and operated by Basotho.

The greater part of Basutoland is very mountainous and in this area all transport is by pack animals. A new mountain motor road running eastwards from Maseru into the mountains has been constructed in order to open up the interior and facilitate its development and administration. The road has reached a point 80 miles from Maseru.

On the western side of Basutoland a fairly good road system has been developed in the strip of agricultural country running from north to south. The main roads traverse the whole of this area, with branches leading west to the principal points of exit. From the main roads a number of feeder-roads lead towards the foothills.
Principal Occupations

Basutoland is a pastoral country without factories or industrial undertakings, and since the produce of the land is insufficient to support the total population, many able-bodied Basotho leave the Territory each year to take up employment in the Republic of South Africa. The Government protects labourers by forbidding recruitment for any mine or other concern where the conditions of work are not good. All labourers recruited by agents in Basutoland for work outside the Territory have to be attested before an official and the contract must be according to Government Regulations.

The diamond diggings at Letsérq-la-Terac though at present haphazard and inefficiently carried out, have proved a very useful source of income to enterprising Basotho. The Government also derived useful revenue in the form of taxes from the sale of diamonds.
Main Crops and Products

So far there has been little evidence of any exploitable mineral deposits in the Territory. Agriculture and animal husbandry are Basutoland's main sources of wealth. The chief exports at present are wheat, wool and mohair. It is estimated that 1,000,000 acres are under cultivation.

The soils in the mountain areas are of basaltic origin, while those in the lowlands are derived mainly from underlying cave sandstone. In the lowlands the soil has been cropped continuously for upwards of 80 years. Due to the absence of fuel, practically all cattle manure is burnt, so that little or no organic matter is returned to the land. Thus with increasing population, both human and livestock, excessive demands have been made on the soil, which has lost its structure and become subject to serious erosion. Controlled grazing has brought back a grass cover and has effectively halted the loss of soil by storm water run off in the mountain areas. In 1961 some 1,252,000 trees were planted as part of soil conservation measures.
There has been no appreciable change in the total arable acreage estimated at 1,000,000 acres.

Weather conditions had a decisive effect on both winter and summer crops during 1964/65. The main Lowland summer crops, maize and sorghum, were adversely affected by drought and frost while the winter crops, peas and wheat, benefited greatly from the heavy snow which fell in June 1964. The wheat crop of about 100,000 bags was an all-time record. Owing to the prolonged drought, yields of mountain peas and wheat were below average.

Soil conservation and reclamation work has progressed at a slower rate than in the past. Some 25 miles of fencing was erected and about 140,000 trees were planted. Twenty dams were completed in the two Improvement Areas of Thaba-Phatsoa and Mejametalana. Some of the stored water is being used for vegetable production.
There are about 1,400,000 up-graded merino sheep, 750,000 Angora goats and 400,000 cattle in the territory.
Trade

Basutoland has no industries and imports the consumer goods and capital items it needs as well as a certain amount of agricultural produce and livestock. Exports consist almost entirely of agricultural commodities and livestock, the main exports being wool and mohair, followed by wheat, sorghum and cattle. The value of imports usually exceeds the value of exports but the adverse balance is partly offset by the export of labour to the Republic of South Africa.

BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE
Area

Bechuanaland has not been surveyed as a whole, but its area is estimated at 222,000 square miles. The headquarters is at Gaberones, the new capital, to which the administration moved from Mafeking during 1965.
Population
The census taken in 1964 showed a population of 535,275 Africans, 3,921 Europeans, 3,489 Euroafricans and 382 Asians. The 1964 Census of total population was 543,105.

The population is not evenly distributed, the bulk of the people living in the eastern part of the country with one fairly large tribe occupying the north-western corner. The central, western and southern areas of the territory, consisting for the most part of the Kalahari Desert, are extremely sparsely inhabited.

The Batswana tribes tend to live in large villages or towns; they are Bakgatla (32,118), Bakwena (73,088), Bangwaketse (71,289), Bamalete (13,848), Bamangwato (201,007), Barolong (10,688), Batawana (42,399), Batlokwa (3,735). About one-half of the population lives in towns of more than 1,000 inhabitants.
Principal Towns
The main business centres are Lobatsi (population 7,639), Gaberones (about 5,000) and Francistown (9,479).
Other large towns are:—Serowe (34,182), Kanye (34,045), Molepolole (29,625); Mochudi (17,712); Mahalapye (13,199); Moshupa (12,892); Thamaga (10,777); and Ramoutsa (10,549).
Geographical Features

Bechuanaland is bounded on the south by Cape Province, on the south-east by the Transvaal, on the north-east by Rhodesia, on the north by the Caprivi Zipfel and on the west by South West Africa. It lies between latitudes 18° and 37° south and longitudes 20° and 29° 50' east. The Kalahari Desert extends over much of the territory west and south of Ngamiland as far as latitude 27°S. This is not a desert in the commonly accepted sense of the word, but consists of a vast expanse of undulating sand-belts with outcrops of limestone. In these limestone belts water is sometimes found at depths from 30 to 100 feet and it is possible that under the deep overburden of sand there may exist untapped reservoirs. The northern
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

and south-western corners of the Kalahari have the least vegetation and most sand dunes, but elsewhere the grasses are excellent, and this is the natural habitat of the aboriginal Bushmen. Large areas are wooded like parklands with camel-thorn and other indigenous trees.

In the north-western part of Bechuanaland the Okovango River, flowing inland from Angola, widens into a delta from which flood-waters, in seasons of heavy rainfall, flow as far south-east as the Makarikari salt-lake and south and south-west into Lake Ngami. The Chobe River, also flowing from Angola, inundates a large area in the north before flowing into the Zambesi 60 miles west of the Victoria Falls.
The mean altitude is 3,300 feet and in the eastern portion occasional elevations, e.g. at Kanye, Serowe and Ghanzi, reach 4,000 to 5,000 feet.
Bechuanaland as a whole is a natural game-reserve for most species of the fauna of Africa and Government policy aims at their preservation.
Climate
The climate is sub-tropical (latitude 22°S. passes through the centre of the country) but varies with latitude and altitude.
The average annual rainfall is 18 inches, varying from 27 inches in the north to 9 inches or less in the western Kalahari.

In the higher parts, days are pleasantly warm and the nights cold during the winter, but the summer is hot. In August seasonal winds blow from the west coast and, dessicated by the Kalahari, carry sand and dust across the whole country.
The atmosphere throughout the year is very dry and this helps somewhat to mitigate the high temperature.
History

In 1884 the British Government appointed a Deputy Commissioner to the Bechuana tribes and in 1885 the whole of the Bechuanaland was, with the concurrence of Khama and other principal chiefs, proclaimed to be under the protection of Her Majesty The Queen. The southern part of the territory, which included Mafeking, Vryburg and Kuruman, was at the same time constituted a Crown Colony with the title British Bechuanaland. In 1885 British Bechuanaland was annexed to Cape Colony, subsequently the Cape Province of the Republic of South Africa. The northern part, known as the Bechuanaland Protectorate, continued to be administered by the Government of the United Kingdom.

The South Africa Act of Union of 1909, which established the Union of South Africa, included provisions for the possible inclusion in South Africa of the three territories of Basutoland, Bechuanaland and Swaziland, which were administered by the High Commissioner for South Africa.

When the South African Constitution was being drawn up the Chiefs in Basutoland, Bechuanaland and Swaziland objected to any scheme which would bring their territories under the rule of South Africa. Assurances were given that no immediate change would be made in the administration of these territories, but provision was made for the possible eventual transfer subject to certain conditions for the protection of African rights embodied in the Act.
After 1909, successive South African Governments asked for the three territories to be trans-

ferred to South Africa and successive British Governments reiterated the pledge made during the passage of the 1909 South Africa Bill through Parliament, that the ultimate responsibility in the question of a decision about transfer rested with the British Government and that no such transfer could take place until the wishes of the inhabitants of the territories had been considered. No requests for transfer have been received recently.

The administration of the territory is governed by various Orders in Council and Proclamations, of which the most important was the Order in Council of Her Majesty Queen Victoria dated 9th May 1891. That Order empowered the then High Commissioner for South Africa to exercise on Her Majesty's behalf all the powers and jurisdiction which Her Majesty had or might have within the Protectorate and to that end authorized him to take or cause to be taken all such measures as he might think expedient, subject to such instructions as he might from time to time receive from Her Majesty or through a Secretary of State. Other provisions of the Order empowered the High Commissioner to appoint administrative and judicial officers and to assign their functions to them subject to the preservation of his own powers in their entirety and to provide, by proclamation from time to time, for the administration of justice, the raising of revenue and the peace, order and good government of all persons within the Protectorate, including the prohibition and punishment of acts tending to disturb the public peace.

In issuing these proclamations the High Commissioner was instructed by the Order in Council to respect any Native laws and customs by which the civil relations of any Native chiefs, tribes or population under Her Majesty's protection were at that time (viz., in May 1891) regulated, except in so far as the same might be incompatible with the due exercise of Her Majesty's power and jurisdiction or which were repugnant to humanity.

The Order required the High Commissioner to publish his proclamations in the Gazette and reserved to Her Majesty the right to disallow any such proclamation. The Order also provided that, subject to any proclamation lawfully issued by the High Commissioner, any jurisdiction exercisable otherwise than under this Order in Council of 1891, whether by virtue of any Statute or Order in Council, or of any treaty, or otherwise, should remain in full force. Her Majesty reserved the power to revoke, alter, add to or amend this Order at any time.

On 28th July 1960 the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations announced his approval of proposals for a new Constitution. This was promulgated in the Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) Order in Council, 1960, which provided for a Legislative Council with power to legislate for the territory, subject to certain reserve powers remaining with the High Commissioner.

The Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) (Amendment) Order 1963 created, in place of the former post of Resident Commissioner, the post of Her Majesty's Commissioner equivalent in status to that of a Governor. It also empowered the Commissioner to assent to legislation and made him directly responsible to the Secretary of State. The post of High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland, was abolished on 1st August 1964.
The Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations and for the Colonies announced on

2nd June 1964 the British Government's general agreement with the unanimous proposals for the revision of the constitution formulated during the second half of 1963 at discussions initiated by the then Resident Commissioner in which representatives of all political parties and other important groups in Bechuanaland participated.
Constitution

The Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) Order 1965, which became effective in March 1965, provides for a Cabinet consisting of the Prime Minister, Deputy Prime Minister and up to five other Ministers drawn from the Legislative Assembly. The latter consists of a Speaker, 31 elected members, four special members elected by the elected members and the Attorney-General (who may speak but not vote). A single voters' roll based on universal adult suffrage provides for the election of the 31 members from one-member constituencies. There is a House of Chiefs consisting of the Chiefs of the eight principal tribes and four other members elected by and from the sub-Chiefs of the Chobe, Francistown, Ghanzi and Kgalagadi districts with the special functions of considering draft Bills affecting tribal matters as well as having the right to make representations to Government on any issue affecting the interest of the Tribes. Her Majesty's Commissioner acts in accordance with the advice of the Cabinet, subject to his special responsibility for external affairs, defence and security and the public service, and the retention of general reserve executive and legislative powers. A code of fundamental human rights, enforceable by the High Court, is also contained in the Constitution. The Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) (Amendment) Order 1965 varied certain provisions governing Cabinet proceedings.

The first general election was held on 1st March 1965. A total of 140,789 people voted; 28 seats went to the Bechuanaland Democratic Party, (leader Mr. Seretse Khama) and 3 to Bechuanaland Peoples Party (leader Mr. P. Matante). The Botswana Independence Party (leader Mr. M. Mpho) gained no seats.

It was announced in October 1965 that the British Government had agreed that a date in the latter part of 1966 would be appropriate for the territory to become independent and that it was expected that the necessary steps could be taken to enable this to be achieved by 30th September 1966.

A conference was held in London in February 1966 to settle the terms of the independence constitution. Its main task was to consider proposals formulated by the Bechuanaland Government which envisaged the establishment of Bechuanaland as an independent Sovereign Republic, to be known as Botswana. The proposals were agreed, subject to certain modifications, and it was announced that Bechuanaland would become independent under the name of Botswana on 30th September 1966.
Land Tenure

The territory is divided into Crown lands (104,069 square miles); four European blocks of farms, mainly on land near the eastern border granted in perpetuity to the British South African Company in 1895, and eight Tribal Territories: Batawana (35,082 square miles); Bamangwato (44,310 square miles); Batlokwa (67 square miles); Bamalete (167 square miles); Bakgatla (2,798
square miles); Bakwena (14,719 square miles); Bangwaketse (9,921 square miles); and Barolong Farms (433 square miles), where ownership of land is vested in the tribes for allocation by the Chiefs.
Taxation

The main forms of direct taxation are income tax, cattle export tax and local government tax. The principal sources of indirect taxation are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Bechuanaland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on all imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades, businesses and professions. There are also death duties.

Under the Commonwealth Development Act 1963, Bechuanaland received an allocation of £2,100,000. This, together with a supplementary allocation of £560,000 made earlier in 1963 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act, 1959, and the unspent balance of previous allocations, gave the Protectorate approximately £2,716,000 towards development over the period 1963-66. The programme provides for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:
