Education

Communications are difficult, distances great and financial resources far from commensurate with urgent needs. Consequently the administration and promotion of African education are unusually difficult.

Most of the administrative work has devolved upon tribal school committees and in three remote areas education is administered directly by the District Commissioners concerned and the Department of Education.

Primary Education. Enrolled in the 241 African primary schools in Bechuanaland during 1964 were 62,839 pupils of whom approximately 56% were girls. The disparity between the sexes, caused by the absence of boys at cattle-posts and on the land, has had a retarding influence ever since schools were first established.
Secondary Education. Three of the eight secondary schools in the territory are co-educational secondary boarding institutions offering the Cambridge School Certificate course.

These are Moeng College, founded by the late Mr. Tshekedi Khama, a Catholic Mission school, St. Joseph's College, near Gaberones and the London Missionary School, Moeding College, which was opened in 1962. All are grant-aided by Government.

The remaining five are day secondary schools which offer three-year courses leading to the Junior Certificate. Three are administered by Tribal School Committees, one is a grant-aided Catholic Mission school in Francistown and one is an independent unaided school in Serowe.
Total enrolment in 1964 was 1,036.

Trades Training. A Trades School, known now as Bechuanaland Training Centre, built at a cost of £65,000 from Colonial Development and Welfare funds was opened in 1962. It offers courses in building, carpentry and motor mechanics.

Teacher Training. One Teacher Training College is situated in Lobatsi and another in Serowe. Capital cost of the latter, paid for from Colonial Development and Welfare funds amounted to approximately £100,000.
Total output from both Colleges will rise by about 100 per annum in 1966.

Youth Movements. There are about 6,000 Boy Scouts' divided into 123 groups and 6,166 Girl Guides, in 103 groups. Monthly bulletins are issued to all Scout and Guide officers. A government grant of £150 per year to the Girl Guides and £200 per year to the Boy Scouts is applied chiefly to training and travelling expenses.

The total estimated expenditure on educational service from funds controlled by Government, by Tribal Administrations and by missions in the financial year ended 31st March 1964, was £629,881 of which £100,478 was contributed by Colonial Development and Welfare schemes.
Health
There are seven hospitals in Bechuanaland, four of which are Government Hospitals. There are
eight Government Health Centres and five Mission Health Centres. Forty-three Dispensaries are administered by Government, and 40 by Missions. Altogether there are 1,529 beds and cots.
During 1964 there were 491,084 Out-patient attendances. The main diseases were tuberculosis, gastro-enteritis, pneumonia and malaria.

Maternity work, including ante-natal and post-natal care is mainly carried out in the hospitals but there is a special maternity centre administered by the London Missionary Society and supervised by the Government Medical Officer of the district.

The technical staff consists of the Director of Medical Services, the Deputy Director of Medical Services, the Medical Officer of Health, 13 Medical Officers, nine Sanitary Inspectors, the Senior Matron, three Matrons, one Sister-in-Charge, one Sister Tutor and 20 Nursing Sisters. There is a subsidiary nursing staff, trained and semi-trained.

African nurses are trained at both Government and Mission Hospitals. Midwives are trained at the Government Hospital, Francistown, at the Maun Maternity Centre and at the Molepolole Mission Hospital. Nurses undergo a four-year course in general nursing and then receive a year's course in Midwifery.
Communications

The main railway line from Cape Town to the north runs through Bechuanaland, entering it at Ramathlabama, 886 miles from Cape Town, and leaving it at Ramaquabane, 394 miles farther north. The line runs roughly parallel to the eastern boundary of the territory at an average distance from it of about 50 miles. The railway within the territory is owned and operated by Rhodesia Railways. The gauge is 3 feet 6 inches.

There are five miles of tarred roads, and 4,930 miles of gravelled or earth roads, 2,567 of which are maintained by the Public Works Department. A $3.6 million loan made by the International Development Association in 1964 is being used for construction of a new road from Francistown to Maun and for improvement to gravel standard of the main road from the Rhodesian border in the north to the South African border in the south.

There are aerodromes at Francistown and Shakawe and airfields at Gaberones, Ghanzi, Gomare, Kanye, Kasane, Lobatsi, Moshaneng, Mahalapye, Makalamabedi, Maun, Molepolole, Nokaneng, Palapye, Panda-ma-Tenga, Rakops and Serowe, with emergency landing grounds at a number of other points.

BECHUANALAND

It is estimated that a further 30,000 Batswana work in the Republic of South Africa, mostly on a migratory basis. About 227,000 persons are employed in agriculture, the majority being self-employed cattle and grain farmers, generally at subsistence level.
Main Crops and Products

The main occupation of the Batswana is cattle raising. The number of cattle in 1964 was 1,346,533, of which 15,050 animals and 108,000 carcases were exported. There were also 136,847 sheep, 378,489 goats and 2,110 pigs. Seasonal lack of water and grazing and indifferent animal husbandry are the chief handicaps to the expansion of the industry.
Irregular rainfall causes occasional crop failures. The principal crops are sorghum and beans.

Active prospecting work is being carried out by several mining companies in different areas, and there are prospects of development of copper and soda ash deposits in the north. The asbestos mine at Moshaneng in the Banwaketse Tribal Territory closed down in May 1965. Manganese is mined in the Balamete Tribal Territory.

The chief imports are maize and maize meal, cattle, general merchandise, foodstuffs, textiles, motor vehicles and spares.

BERMUDA
Situation and Area

The "Bermudas" or "Somers Islands" are situated in the Western Atlantic Ocean, about 570 miles east of Cape Hatteras in North Carolina, and until 1940 had an area of 19.34 square miles. As a result of work done by the United States authorities since 1940 to unite and extend some of the islands with materials dredged from the sea, their total area is now 20.59 square miles, of which 2.30 square miles are leased to the Government of the United States for naval and military bases, leaving 18.29 square miles available to the civil population. The United States Bases include a large airfield which is used by both military and commercial traffic.
Population
The estimated civil population on the 30th June 1965 was 48,355, of whom 17,452 were white and 30,903 coloured.
Principal Towns

The City of Hamilton, the capital since 1815, with a population estimated at about 3,000, is situated on the main island. The town of St. George on the island of St. George was formerly the capital of the Colony. Its population is estimated at about 1,600.
Geographical Features

The Bermudas consist of about 150 coralstone islands and islets, roughly in the form of a fish-hook, along the southern rim of the oval plateau summit, about 22 miles from east to west and 14 miles from north to south, of a steep submarine volcanic mountain which is reputed to be between 14,000 and 15,000 feet in height. The ten principal islands are connected by bridges and form a chain about 22 miles long between its north-east and south-west extremities. These islands vary in width but the main island, which is about 14 miles long, has an average width of about 1 mile; it contains about 9,000 acres of land, the highest point being only 259.4 feet above the sea. All the other islands and the areas reclaimed for the United States Bases aggregate about 4,240 acres.
Climate

The climate is generally mild and humid with a mean annual temperature of 70° F. and average annual maximum and minimum temperatures of 88° F. and 47° F. respectively. The coldest and hottest months are February and August with mean temperatures of 62° F. and 80° F. respectively. The average annual rainfall is 57.9 inches. The climate shows very little variation throughout the Colony.
History

According to a 17th century French cartographer the islands were probably discovered in 1503 by a Spanish mariner, Juan Bermudez, but the Spaniards took no steps to form a settlement and the islands were still entirely uninhabited when in 1609 Admiral Sir George Somers' ship The Sea Venture was wrecked upon one of the sunken reefs while conveying a party of colonists to Virginia. The reports of the beauty and fertility of the land induced the Virginia Company to seek an extension of their charter so as to include the islands within their dominion and this extension was granted by King James I, in 1612 and the first emigrants went out in that year. Shortly afterwards the Virginia Company sold the islands for the sum of £2,000 to a new body of adventurers called "The Governor and Company of the City of London for the Plantation of the Somers Islands", and thenceforward for a considerable time the islands bore that name. In 1684 the charter of the Bermuda Company of London was annulled and government passed to the Crown.
Constitution

Representative government was introduced into the Colony in 1620. Since 1684 the Governors have been appointed by the Crown and the laws of the Colony have been enacted by a local legislature. Until 1888 this legislature consisted of the Governor and the House of Assembly and since then of the Governor, the Legislative Council and the House of Assembly.

The Governor is assisted by an Executive Council consisting of three official members (Colonial Secretary, Colonial Treasurer and the Attorney General), and six unofficial members (increased from five in November, 1962) appointed by the Crown.
The Legislative Council consists of three official members (The Chief Justice as President, the Colonial Secretary and the Attorney General) and eight nominated members.

The House of Assembly consists of thirty-six members elected for a term of five years. Under the provisions of the Parliamentary Act, 1963 the nine parishes, which had previously been represented by four members, were divided into two electoral districts, each represented by two members. The Act requires that voters must be British Subjects of twenty-five years of age or over at the time of registration, and if not possessing Bermudian status, must have been ordinarily resident in the Colony for the whole of the period of three years immediately prior to registration, registration being held every year. Candidates for election must qualify as electors and, in addition, must possess Bermudian status. Electors who own the freehold estate in land in Bermuda of an area of not less than 2,000 square feet will now qualify for an additional vote. Formerly the franchise had been limited to those persons possessing freehold property of not less than £60 in value and, prior to 1944, only males were permitted to vote or to seek election. The 1963 Act was amended in 1966 by reducing the voting age to 21 years and by abolishing the additional property vote.

At the General Election held on the 16th May 1963 86.5% of the registered electors voted. The Progressive Labour Party, the first political party to emerge in Bermuda, was successful in getting six of its nine candidates elected. In 1964 twenty-four of the remaining members joined together to form the United Bermuda Party.
Digitized by Google
BERMUDA
Members of the Executive and Legislative Councils and of the House of Assembly are paid 24s. for each day's attendance.

A number of Government Departments are controlled by Executive Boards which consist of unofficials appointed by the Governor. The head of the Department acts in an advisory capacity. The chairmen of most of the major Boards are members of the House of Assembly.
Taxation

There is no direct taxation. The revenue of the Colony is mostly raised from Customs duties. The revenue of the corporation of Hamilton is derived from wharfage, shed tax, rents and local taxation assessed at the rate of 16s. on every £100 of real estate and personal property. The corporation of St. George's obtains its revenue in like manner, the rates being 13s. 6d. on each £100. The nine parishes, which administer the general parochial affairs of the poor law, levy rates, and under the Liquor Licence Act licensees pay a certain sum which varies according to the class of licence held.
Public Finance
In 1964 £4,191,143 of revenue was derived from Customs duties.

Currency

A new coin, the Bermuda Crown, was issued in February 1965. All other coins in circulation are British currency, which is legal tender. There is no limit to the legal tender of British silver. Bermuda Government £10, £5, £1, 10s. and 5s. currency notes to the value of £1,799,945 10s. 0d., were in circulation on 31st December 1964.
Development
The total acreage available for agriculture fell again during 1964, due to the continuing increase in population, and by the year's end was 1,020 acres.
The re-afforestation scheme, started in 1949 with the object of replacing the dead Bermuda cedars, continued on a reduced scale with only 4,700 trees being felled and replaced.

The Parks Division, formed in 1956, continued to care for the many small wayside parks bordering public roads, gardens, school playing fields and grounds, Government House Grounds and the Botanical Gardens, in all comprising some 385 acres.
Public Works
The following is a brief summary of work carried out:
Buildings. —The new wing of the King Edward VII Memorial Hospital was completed, and it was opened in June 1965.
A ten-year development plan for the Civil Air Terminal was drawn up, and all alterations will now be carried out in compatibility with this plan.
A central site was selected for a new and up-to-date General Post Office, which is to cost an estimated £421,000. Plans and design were approved in June 1965.
Minor improvements were carried out at Government House and at the Local Forces Camp in Warwick, and a number of Government offices were fitted with air-conditioners.
Work continued on the £4,000,000 school building programme.

Aerial Survey. —Checking of the preliminary survey maps began in mid-1964, but receipt of these is proving slower than expected. It is anticipated that the full range of completed maps will not be available before 1967.
Roads. —Widening and resurfacing was carried out. No new roads were constructed.
Bridges. —£21,000 was voted for the repair of Flatts Bridge. The sum includes the raising of the span by 18 inches, and this will enable larger boats to enter Harrington Sound.
Sea Water Distillation Plant. —The plant became fully operative in May 1965 and, in spite of a number of technical setbacks, had produced nearly 6,000,000 gallons of water by the end of the year.

Libraries, Museums, etc.
The Bermuda Library, with adult, children's and music departments in Hamilton, was greatly enlarged during 1957 and now accommodates the interesting collection of the Bermuda Historical

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Society. It has branches in St. George's and Somerset and a distribution service to the schools of the Colony.

The Bermuda Government Aquarium with adjoining live zoological collection, which was also considerably enlarged and much improved, has a museum of local natural history and other exhibits. The Bermuda Historical Society has a second museum in St. George containing items of local historical interest.
Health

The level of health in the Colony continued to be high and Government services are chiefly engaged in preventive measures. The Public Health Department is responsible for the inspection of milk and meat, garbage collection and disposal, rat control, mosquito control, etc. It is also responsible for pre-natal and pre-school health services.

The King Edward VII Memorial Hospital is privately run, but receives an annual Government grant-in-aid, amounting in 1965 to £310,000. The Mental Hospital is attended by Government Medical Officers and Health Officers. On the 1st October 1960, a Health Insurance Scheme for Government employees and their dependants was inaugurated. The scheme is compulsory for Government employees and optional for their dependants. Contributions are at the rate of £5 2s. 0d. per annum for an officer only, £10 4s. 0d. per annum for an officer and one dependant and £15 12s. 0d. for an officer and two or more dependants. The Government contributes equal amounts into the fund.
During 1964 total government expenditure on Health Services including the Mental Hospital was £573,293 compared with approximately £176,841 in 1955.
Communications
The air terminal is at Kindley Field, St. George's Island.

Six airlines, i.e. B.O.A.C.-Cunard, P.A.A., Eastern, Air Canada, Qantas, and Iberia, provide Bermuda with scheduled services to Britain, Spain, Canada, the United States, the Bahamas, the Caribbean and West Indies, Central and South America, the Pacific and Australia. A total of 3,259 flights through the Colony were recorded in 1964.

Direct and indirect seaborne passenger and cargo services are maintained with varying frequency and regularity with all parts of the world and merchant shipping of 34 nations made a total of 820 calls at Bermuda in 1964.

The Colony has 132 miles of tarmac roads with 8½ miles in Hamilton and 4½ miles in St. George's. On the 31st January 1965 there were 21,987 vehicles licensed in the Colony comprising 7,447 private cars, 512 taxis and 85 buses, 1,237 motor trucks, 12,176 auto and auxiliary cycles and 530 miscellaneous vehicles.
There is no railway in Bermuda.
Broadcasting

There are two broadcasting stations, both commercial, one operated by the Bermuda Broadcasting Company Limited, using the call sign Z.B.M., the other operated by Capital Broadcasting Company Limited, using the call sign ZFB.
The first named company was formed in 1943 and started broadcasting in 1946 on 1240 kc/s with a power output of 250 watts. In 1953 a second
station, Z.B.M.2 was added to the network and in 1962 power on both stations was increased to 1,000 watts and an F.M. station Z.B.M.-F.M. was inaugurated.
The Company have also operated since January 1958, a television service known as Z.B.M.-T.V.
The latter named company was formed in 1962 and commenced broadcasting the same year with a power output of 1,000 watts. In July 1965 they opened a Television Station (ZFB-TV).
The Colony thus has four radio stations and two television stations. All are commercially financed, and neither radio nor television receiving licences are required.
Principal Occupations

The tourist trade provides employment for a majority of the population. Estimated numbers in some significant occupations are: Hotels/Guest Houses 2,975; commercial 3,700; construction 1,950; agriculture and gardening 670; general labourers (male) 1,650; transport 950; teachers 540.

Main Crops and Products
The main crops are vegetables (including potatoes), bananas and citrus fruits. Easter Lilies are cultivated for bulbs, flower buds and stems. There is considerable milk and egg production.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The old Planned Productions Marketing Programme was discontinued in mid-1964, due to lack of support by farmers: an ever increasing number were dealing with retailers direct and the Programme had become superfluous.
The wholesale Marketing Centre continued in operation and made a trading profit of £1,835 in 1964. Purchases of agricultural produce totalled £47,932 whilst sales realised £49,356.

BRITISH ANTARCTIC TERRITORY
Area

The British Antarctic Territory was created on the 3rd March 1962 and, with the exception of the island of South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands, includes the area previously known as the Falkland Islands Dependencies. The Territory lies between 20–80 degrees of west longitude and south of the 60th parallel of latitude. Within these bounds lie the South Orkneys, the South Shetlands and the Antarctic Peninsula, together with all adjacent islands and the land mass extending to the South Pole.
Population

The British population of the Territory consists solely of male scientists and technicians who man the British Antarctic Survey scientific stations. During the winter of 1965 the total number was 86. In the summer months when relief personnel arrive, together with summer field workers, this figure may almost double.
No exact number of other nationals working in the area is known.
Principal Towns
There are no towns in the Territory. Scientific and field stations are situated on various islands and parts of the mainland.
Digitized by Google
BRITISH ANTARCTIC TERRITORY
Geographical Features

The majority of the islands in the Territory are wild and rugged with many glaciers. The Antarctic Peninsula is mountainous, the highest peak being Mount Andrew Jackson (13,700 feet). There is a snow covered plateau which extends along the peninsula, but declines from 7,000 feet in the south to about 4,000 feet in the north. The main continental area is covered by ice and fringed by floating ice shelves generally about 800 feet thick.
Climate
The Territory has a rigorous polar climate.
History
The main island group of the South Shetlands was discovered and taken possession of by Captain W. Smith in 1819, whilst the South Orkney Islands owe their discovery to Captain G. Powell in 1821.

The Antarctic Peninsula was discovered in 1820 by Edward Bransfield, R.N., and taken into the possession of Great Britain by John Biscoe in 1832. The penetration of what is now known as the Weddell Sea and the final discovery of the Continental Land Masses to the South and East of Antarctic Peninsula can be attributed to many great explorers—Cook, Weddell, Ross and Bruce to name but a few. Many of the Islands within the Territory were used as temporary bases for the early sealing and whaling expeditions, but at Deception Island of the South Shetlands Island Group a more permanent and more active whaling station was in operation from 1906 to 1931. During this time a magistrate was in permanent residence for the summer months.

In the 1943–44 season, under the code name of "Operation Tabarin", the first of what are now known as the British Antarctic Survey bases were established at Deception Island and Port Lockroy. Since then the occupation of bases has been continuous and the number so occupied now stands at (6) with a summer operations base at Fossil Bluff; their locations are as follows:
SIGNY ISLAND—South Orkneys $60^{\circ}43'$ South $45^{\circ}36'$ West.
DECEPTION ISLAND—South Shetlands $62^{\circ}59'$ South $60^{\circ}34'$ West.
GALINDEZ ISLAND } West Coast of Graham ARGENTINE ISLANDS } Land $65^{\circ}15'$ South $64^{\circ}16'$ West.
ADELAIDE ISLAND—$67^{\circ}46'$ South $68^{\circ}55'$ West. West Coast of Graham Land.
FOSSIL BLUFF—King George VI Sound.
HALLEY BAY—Caird Coast $75^{\circ}31'$ South $26^{\circ}36'$ West.

Other bases have been established, but are at present unoccupied; they were known as:
Base A—Port Lockroy, Wiencke Islands, Palmer Archipelago.
Base C—Cape Geddes, Laurie Island, South Orkneys.
Base D—Hope Bay, Trinity Peninsula.
Base G—Admiralty Bay, King George Island, South Shetlands.
Base J—Prospect Point, Graham Coast.
Base N—Anvers Island, Palmer Archipelago.
Base O—Danco Coast, Graham Land.
Base V—View Point.
Base W—Detaille Islets, Loubet Coast, Graham Land.
Base Y—Horeshoe Island, Marguerite Bay.
Constitution
The Territory is administered by the High Commissioner resident in Stanley, Falkland Islands (see page 105).
A Court of Appeal was set up on the 1st July 1965 for the purpose of hearing and determining appeals from the courts of the territory.
Land Policy
The policy is guided by the Antarctic Treaty.
Taxation
All British scientists and technicians are taxed under the Falkland Islands Income Tax Ordinance.
Public Finance
For the years 1960–61 (see under Falkland Islands ). Until the year ended 30th June 1962 British Antarctic Territory finances were embodied in those of the Falkland Islands Dependencies.
(a) Includes H.M.G. grants.

In 1964–65 the revised estimate of revenue totalled £39,409. In addition, the sum of £724,572 was expected to be received from Her Majesty's Government as a contribution towards the cost of running the British Antarctic Survey (ex Falkland Islands Dependencies Survey), Antarctic Bases and Ships.
Currency
Currency (when used) is British coinage and Falkland Islands £5 notes, £1 notes and 10s. notes which are interchangeable with sterling on par.
Communications

Communications between the Territory, the Colony of the Falkland Islands and the United Kingdom are maintained by the British Antarctic Survey's Royal Research Ships the 'John Biscoe' and the 'Shackleton' aided by the chartered vessel m.v. 'Kista Dan'. These ships operate only from November to May.

Between these months the Antarctic Bases are relieved, resupplied and restaffed. Radio communication for the passing of scientific and administrative data is linked between Antarctic Bases and the British Antarctic Survey in Stanley, Falkland Islands.

BRITISH GUIANA
Area
British Guiana lies on the north-east coast of the South American Continent. Its area is 83,000 square miles, divided into three counties as follows :

Square miles
Essequibo .. .. .. 61,660
Berbice .. .. .. 16,920
Demerara .. .. .. 4,420
Population
At 31st December 1964 the population was 638,030. Estimates of racial groups in 1964 were:

East Indians .. .. .. 320,070
African descent .. .. 199,830
Mixed descent .. .. .. 75,990
Portuguese .. .. .. 6,380
Europeans other than Portuguese .. .. .. 2,420
Chinese .. .. .. .. 3,910
Amerindians .. .. .. 29,430
The provisional number of births and deaths in 1964 were 25,393 and 4,945 respectively. The infant mortality rate in 1963 was 55 per 1,000 live births.
Principal Towns

The City of Georgetown, Demerara, is the capital and with its environs has a population of approximately 162,000. Population in the city proper numbers about 78,000. New Amsterdam, Berbice, has a population of 15,000 approximately. Mackenzie-Wismar-Christianburg, a bauxite mining township up the Demerara River, has a population of 20,000.
Geographical Features

The Colony may be divided into three zones, viz., the low-lying coastland, the mountain region and the intermediate region. The coastland comprises a low-lying, swampy strip of marine alluvium which varies in width from about ten miles in the west to about 40 miles in the east. Its flat, grassy savannahs would mostly be inundated during the rainy season but for artificial drainage and sea defences. Almost the whole of the population and agricultural activity of the Colony is concentrated between the Pomeroon River and Corentyne River along this strip. The mountain region, known as the Pakaraima Range, lies along the western boundary of the Colony. Mount Roraima, a plateau 22 square miles in extent and over 9,000 feet above sea level, is the highest elevation. The intermediate region, the largest area of the Colony, lies mainly to the east and south. It is almost entirely forest clad. The total area under forest is approximately 70,000 square miles.

There are many large rivers which with their numerous tributaries form a vast network of waterways. All the large rivers are impeded above the tideway by rapids, cataracts and falls. Kaieteur, one of the largest single-drop waterfalls in the world, is situated on the Potaro River. It is nearly five times the height of Niagara and has a vertical drop of 741 feet with a further 80 feet of rapids and falls. Its width varies from 350 to 400 feet.
Climate
The climate is pleasant for the greater part of the year; it is hot only in the summer months. The mean temperature is 80°F. with a daily range of about 18°F. on the coast and a slightly wider

range inland. There are two wet and two dry seasons in the coastal regions—the long wet season usually from April to August and the long dry season from September to mid-November. This is followed by wet weather until the end of January, and the short dry season until April. The mean annual rainfall is 93 inches, but there are wide variations in the rainfall over the region, ranging from 80 to 100 inches along the coast and falling as low as 60 inches in the savannahs of the interior where the dry season is October to February and the wet season May to August. The nights are uniformly cool.
Slight earth tremors are felt occasionally, but no damage is caused.
History
There have been some discrepancies in dates and other matters in the various historical accounts but the following brief statement is based upon what is taken to be the most authentic version.

In history there are really five Guianas—Spanish Guiana (now Venezuela), Portuguese Guiana (now Brazil), Dutch Guiana (now Surinam), French Guiana and British Guiana. A navigable river was so important to the Europeans who first visited the coastline between the Amazon and the Orinoco that they gave the Amerindian name of the river to the surrounding countryside. Thus the three counties of British Guiana are Essequibo, Demerara and Berbice, and the areas known as Rupununi, Mazaruni, Potaro, Cuyuni, Pomeroon and Corentyne take their names from the rivers that flow through them. Guiana is an Amerindian word meaning "Land of Waters."

The coastline of the country between the Amazon and Orinoco was first traced by Spanish sailors in 1499 and 1500 and the first European settlements were almost certainly Spanish or Portuguese. The Dutch established a settlement on the Pomeroon in 1581 but were evicted by Spanish and Indians (probably in 1596) and retired to Kyk-over-al up the Essequibo. In 1627 Dutch merchants settled on the Berbice. The Dutch West Indian Company, formed in 1621, controlled these settlements.

The first English attempt at settlement in the area lasted from 1604–06, on the Oyapock River (now French Guiana). Fresh ventures in 1609 and 1629 failed to establish a permanent settlement. Lord Willoughby, famous in the early history of Barbados, founded a settlement in Surinam in 1651 but this was captured by the Dutch in 1667. In October of the same year it was recaptured by a British expedition from Barbados, the leaders being unaware of the signing of the Treaty of Breda in the previous July. It was mid-1668 before the Dutch finally obtained possession of Surinam in accordance with the terms of that Treaty. In effect Surinam was exchanged for New York, though this was not explicit in the terms of the Treaty.

The Dutch retained their hold on the territory with more or less firmness, now yielding to England and now to France or Portugal, until 1796 when during the war of the French Revolution it was captured by a British fleet from Barbados. The territory was restored to the Dutch in 1802, but in the following year retaken by Great Britain and finally ceded to her in 1814, exclusive of Surinam.
Constitution
Following the decisions of the Constitutional Conference held in London in March 1960, the British Guiana (Constitution) Order in Council,
Digitized by Google
BRITISH GUIANA

1961 was passed, providing for a new constitution giving full internal self-government to British Guiana. The new constitution, which came into effect on 18th July 1961, provided for a bi-cameral legislature—a Legislative Assembly of 35 members, elected by universal adult suffrage, and a nominated Senate of 13 members.
These provisions were amended by the British Guiana (Constitution) Order in Council, 1964. (See below.)

The Executive body, called the Council of Ministers, is presided over by the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister is the member of the Legislative Assembly who, in the judgment of the Governor, is best able to command the confidence of a majority of the members of that Chamber. The Council of Ministers is responsible to the Legislative Assembly.

The Governor must exercise all his powers in accordance with the advice of the Council of Ministers except where otherwise expressly stated (the notable exceptions are in respect of defence and external affairs).
A Bill of Rights incorporated in the Constitution enumerates in detail rights to equality, personal freedom and property and provides for their enforcement by the Supreme Court.

The Constitution also provides for the establishment of Judicial, Public and Police Service Commissions, which will ultimately have executive responsibility for appointments, discipline and dismissals in the Services for which they are responsible. Judges of the Supreme Court may be removed only for inability or misconduct and then only by the Governor on the advice of the Judicial Committee of the Privy Council, which itself can be approached only on the recommendations of a locally appointed Judicial tribunal.

Arising out of a resolution passed by the British Guiana Legislature in October 1961, Her Majesty's Government announced in January 1962 its willingness to hold a Constitutional Conference in London in May 1962 to discuss the date and the arrangements to be made for the achievement of independence by British Guiana. The Conference was held in October, having been delayed to await the report of the Commission of Inquiry on the Disturbances which took place in Georgetown in February. Delegates from each of the three political parties represented in the Legislative Assembly attended the Conference. As no agreement could be reached on the terms of a Constitution for Independence, the Conference was adjourned to allow for further discussions between the parties in British Guiana. Since these discussions did not lead to agreement, the Secretary of State reconvened the Conference in October, 1963.

At the resumed conference the leaders of the three political parties reported that they had failed to reach agreement between themselves on the terms of a constitution for independence, and asked the British Government to settle on its own authority all the outstanding political issues. The Secretary of State announced his decisions on 31st October at the closing session of the Conference. The main decisions were:
(a) The Constitution must provide the strongest safeguards to protect minorities;
(b) The electoral system would be changed to proportional representation on a party list system;
(c) The voting age would remain at 21 years;
(d) Elections on the new basis would be held as soon as possible under the supervision of a Special Commissioner from outside British Guiana appointed by the British Government;
(e) After these elections a conference could be convened to settle any remaining constitutional issues and to fix a date for independence;
(f) Documents to implement the decisions would be made available in draft to the leaders before receiving the Secretary of State's final approval;

(g) Additional security forces would be raised locally before independence under the direction of the Governor, who would endeavour to ensure that recruits were not drawn predominantly from any one racial group.

On 25th September 1964 the bi-cameral legislature of the 1961 Constitution was dissolved and by proclamation on the same date the British Guiana (Constitution) Order 1964 was brought into operation. That Order provides for the establishment of a uni-cameral legislature comprising a House of Assembly of 53 members elected by proportional representation. The life of the new legislature is four years unless earlier dissolved. A general election was held on the 7th December 1964, the People's Progressive Party winning 24 seats, the People's National Congress 22 seats and the United Force 7 seats. A P.N.C./U.F. coalition government was formed, with Mr. L. F. S. Burnham, leader of the P.N.C., as Premier and Mr. P. S. D'Aguiar, leader of the U.F. as Minister of Finance.

At the British Guiana Independence Conference held in London in November 1965 it was agreed that British Guiana should become a sovereign independent state under the name of Guyana on the 26th May 1966.

On the 22nd December 1965 two further amendments were made to the Constitution by an Order of the Queen in Council. The first provides that nothing in Article 11(1) of the Constitution (which prohibits discrimination on grounds of race etc.) shall prevent appointments to the police force being made in accordance with the recommendations of the Commission of Inquiry on racial problems in the Public Service instituted by the International Commission of Jurists. The second substitutes the title of Prime Minister for that of Premier.
Land Policy
Land in the Colony falls within three categories:
(i) Freehold : (a) alienated by the Dutch prior to 1803 ; (b) alienated by the Crown ;
(ii) Crown land—about 90% of the area of the Colony ;
(iii) Colony land, acquired from private owners and the Crown for public purposes.

Crown land may be alienated or leased to any individual with the consent of the Governor on the advice of the Minister of Lands, Mines and Forests; in the case of Colony land with the consent of the Council of Ministers. Absentee lessees must have a legal representative in the Colony. Alienation of Crown Land is restricted to leases for periods not exceeding 25 years.
Taxation
The main heads of taxation are Customs, excise and licences, income tax, stamp duties, estate duties, acreage tax, duty on transport and mortgages.

Income tax rates range from 6 cents (for the first $1,200 (£250)) to 70 cents on each $ of chargeable income in the case of individuals. Companies, other than Mutual Life Insurance companies, pay 45%; Mutual Life Insurance companies 25%.
Public Finance
* Total Colony revenue and expenditure.
In 1964, £4,670,159 in revenue came from Income Tax while Customs and Excise produced £6,744,855. In the same year the main heads of expenditure were:

Social Services .. .. £4,440,006
Administration including Law and Order .. .. £3,439,796
Public Works .. .. £1,607,296
Economic Development .. .. £950,743
The Public Debt at the end of 1964 was £26,520,629.
Currency

New currency notes brought into circulation on 15th November 1965 are issued by the Central Bank of Guyana, which began operations on that date. Currency notes are of denominations $1, $5, $10 and $20; coins are 50 cents, 25 cents, 5 cents, 2 cents and 1 cent. The Currency is exchangeable with sterling at par. The Guyanese $ has a fixed equivalent value of 4s. 2d., that is $4.80 to £.
Development Plans

A Two-Year Development Programme, 1954-55, was launched early in 1954. Expenditure fell short of the estimate of $44 million but public investment over the period stimulated economic activity throughout the colony. The unexpended balances from the Two-Year Programme were incorporated into a Five-Year Development Programme for the period 1955 to 1960.

That Programme was designed mainly to improve and extend basic developmental facilities, e.g., drainage and irrigation, roads, internal transport services and telecommunications; to provide for the establishment of comprehensive schemes of rural electrification and land settlement and for an improvement and expansion of social services, in the fields of health, housing and education. It was financed partly from Colony funds, derived from loans and accumulated surplus revenue balances and partly from Colonial Development and Welfare grants. Expenditure on development projects was as follows:
Arising from recommendations made by the International Bank Mission after their visit to the Colony in 1953, for the planning and execution

of the Development Programme, a Credit Corporation was established to administer credits for the encouragement and development of agriculture, industry, forestry, fisheries, rural and urban housing. The total amount loaned up to the end of 1964 was $28,326,547; repayments totalled $21,999,075.

Regional Development Committees and Area Sub-Committees have been established in each of the twelve administrative districts to assist in general development activities, especially to promote aided self-help schemes and to advise the Credit Corporation on applications for loans.
An Industrial Development Committee was set up in 1957.
A Five-Year Development Programme ended in December 1964. Expenditure amounted to $89.3 million.

A Seven-Year (1966-1972) Development Programme has been prepared with the help of a team of local and foreign experts headed by Professor Sir Arthur Lewis of Princeton University, U.S.A. A Development Secretariat has also been established.
Other Development

Development is also being undertaken by the Commonwealth Development Corporation. The Corporation has concessions for the large-scale development of timber in the area known as the Bartica Triangle, and operates a mill at Houston valued at over £600,000. The Corporation has also furnished capital on medium and short-term loan for the Rice Development Company.

Technical assistance in many fields of development work is being provided by the United States Agency for International Development (formerly the International Co-operation Administration) whose personnel stationed in the Colony include a Chief A.I.D. representative and administrative staff, and who have from time to time provided specialists in Housing, Community Development, Geology, Agriculture, Land Settlement, Drainage and Irrigation, Road Construction, Health and Industrial Development.

Technical assistance for development in various fields has been received also from the United Kingdom, from the Governments of Israel, Puerto Rico, Canada, West Germany and U.S.A. and from the United Nations and its agencies. The scale of United Nations Technical Assistance from 1962 to 1964 has been substantial.
Education

There is no standard test for literacy, but using attendance at school for a period of eight years as a criterion for literacy, it is estimated that about 80-85% of the population of British Guiana is literate.
There are 349 primary and all-age schools—Government owned and aided—with a population of 147,839 pupils.
There are 14 Government-aided secondary schools, 13 separate Government-owned secondary schools, and 35 privately owned schools.
Teacher Training

The Government Training College for teachers provides (1) in five regional branches, a one-year in-service course (afternoon and evening), for student-teachers drawn from schools in the immediate area of the regional training centre and (2) a full-time two-year pre-service course for candidates from secondary grammar schools.
Digitized by Google
BRITISH GUIANA

Scholarships are provided for teachers entering the pre-service training centres. The sum of $50,000 has been provided for this under a Government Scholarship Award Scheme with the assistance of U.N.E.S.C.O. In order to assist in carrying out these programmes graduates have been recruited under the provisions of the British and Canadian Overseas Aid Schemes.
The Government Technical Institute continues to train industrial apprentices though it is centreing its efforts on higher level education rather than craft.

The Carnegie School of Home Economics, run by Government, offers for girls between ages 15-20, a three-year full time course covering cookery, dietics, dressmaking, tailoring, hygiene, childcare, and homemaking.

The Guyana School of Agriculture, another prospective faculty of the University of Guyana, absorbs students from both the grammar and comprehensive schools. Established in 1963, it provides courses at different levels for farmers, members of Young Farmers Clubs, and for agricultural technicians and teachers.
It offers a two-year Diploma course in Agriculture and Animal Husbandry for which Government, in addition to scholarships, provides students subsistence.
University Education.

Higher education is offered by the University of Guyana set up in 1963 and providing tuition in Arts, Natural Sciences and Social Sciences. Associated with the University are the Research Divisions of the Departments of Geological Survey and Forestry, which will eventually become Research Institutes of the University.

Until construction of the permanent University buildings, towards which a grant of £350,000 ($1,680,000) has been made by the British Government, is completed, courses are being conducted as evening classes at Queens College. There were 268 students on the roll of the University of Guyana at the end of 1964.
Students Studying Overseas.
The number of students from British Guiana studying overseas on the 1st January, 1962, was as follows:

United Kingdom — 948
Canada — 49
U.S.A. — 239
Jamaica, U.W.I. — 153
The total recurrent expenditure on Education in 1964 was £2,393,423. In 1938, the expenditure was £94,290.
Health
During 1964, the sum of £1,416,189 was spent on Public Health and Medical Care. This figure represents 10 per cent of total expenditure of the territory.
At the beginning of 1962 an outbreak of parathion poisoning occurred and in November an epidemic of poliomyelitis commenced and reached its height at the end of the year.
Among the principal causes of death the group "Diseases of early infancy" has the first place,
followed by pneumonia and bronchitis, diseases of the heart and gastro-enteritis and colitis. Gastro-enteritis is a very prevalent cause of death of infants one year of age and under.

Prevailing diseases include pulmonary tuberculosis, filariasis due to wuchereria bancrofti, typhoid fever, helminthiasis and minor nutritional disorders attributable mainly to a deficiency in the dietary of B Complex vitamins.

Preventive measures provided by Government consist of activities provided through a Division of Environmental Sanitation, Division of Maternal and Child Health, Public Health Nursing Division, Health Education Division and a Filariasis Unit to combat filariasis.
In 1964 there were 32 Health Centres, 23 of which were established after 1960. There are 116 Maternal and Child Health Units in the rural areas.

There were in 1964, seven general hospitals with 1,407 beds; ten private general hospitals with 490 beds; eight cottage hospitals with 86 beds; one Tuberculosis Hospital with 246 beds; one Leprosy Hospital with 354 beds and one Mental Hospital with 834 beds.
Communications

Government operates one International Airport (Atkinson) and maintains at least twenty other land aerodromes throughout the country. Internal air services are provided by Guyana Airways Corporation (previously known as British Guiana Airways (Govt.)) who are also agents for British West Indian Airways and Cruzeiro-do-Sul. External services making connections with world net-work of air routes are provided by British West Indian Airways, Pan American Airways, the K.L.M. Royal Dutch Airlines, Air France and Cruzeiro-do-Sul (Brazil).

There are two Government owned railway lines (single track) both parallel to the coast and totalling about 80 miles in length. Passengers carried in 1964 totalled 3,125,510 and freight totalled 52,247 tons.

There were in 1964, about 320 miles of paved roads with bitumin, concrete or asphalt surface, and about 750 miles of roads consisting of sand or clay embankments surfaced with stone, gravel, burnt earth or unsurfaced. In addition there were 1,000 miles of vehicular trails.
The chief ports are Georgetown, New Amsterdam and Springlands with sub-ports at Mora-whanna and Kaituma.

British Guiana is in direct communication with the United Kingdom, France, Holland, Canada, the U.S.A., the West Indies, Surinam and French Guiana. Services are provided for passengers and cargo principally by Bookers Lines, Harrison Lines, Royal Netherlands Steamship Company, Compagnie Generale Transatlantique, Alcoa and Saguenay Terminals.

The three main rivers, Demerara, Essequibo and Berbice are navigable by ocean-going vessels of moderate draught for about 65, 35 and 100 miles respectively. There are also numerous other rivers which are navigable in their tidal reaches by smaller vessels. There are many long stretches of water in the upper rivers navigable by launches. Ferry services, coastal and river services are also operated by steamers.
Broadcasting

The commercial broadcasting station, Radio Demerara, operates on short and medium waves with a power of 10,000 watts medium wave, and 2,000 watts short wave; another station—the British Guiana Broadcasting Service—which was officially opened in December 1958 operates a 10 kW transmitter on 560 kc/s (536 metres). There were 39,337 licensed holders of radio receiving sets in 1964.
Principal Occupations
The greater part of the population is engaged in agriculture. Estimates of average employment for the following industries are: Sugar field workers 12,451; Sugar milling 3,583; Bauxite 4,110.
At the end of 1964 there were 54 registered workers' organizations and 6 employers' associations.
The Main Crops and Products are sugar, rum, molasses, rice, bauxite, timber, gold and diamonds. In 1964 production was:

BRITISH HONDURAS
Area

British Honduras lies on the Caribbean coast of Central America, bounded on the north and part of the west by Mexico and by Guatemala in the remainder of the west and south. Its land area is about 8,866 square miles, which includes a number of islets, known as "cayes", lying off the coast.
Population

The estimated population at the end of 1964 was 104,450. At the census taken in April 1960 the de facto total population was 90,019. The main racial groups are: Creoles, American Indians (Maya) and Caribs. There are also a number of
persons of East Indian and Spanish descent. The races are, however, now heavily inter-mixed and a great many persons would have considerable difficulty in deciding to which group they belong.

English is the official language and the language taught in the schools. Spanish is also in common use, chiefly by the Indians, whose language it is tending to replace. The term Creole is now commonly used to denote local negroes and persons of negro extraction; it is also used in respect of persons of mixed Spanish and Indian descent

Principal TownsPopulation

Geographical Features

The coastline is, for the most part, flat and swampy, but the country rises gradually towards the interior. The Maya Mountains and the Cockscombs, which reach a height of 3,700 feet, form the backbone of the southern half of the Colony. All of the western (Cayo) District is hilly and includes the Mountain Pine Ridge most of which lies between 1,000 and 2,000 feet above sea level but rises in parts to around 3,000 feet. The northern districts are also hilly except towards the coast but contain considerable areas of low tableland. There are seventeen principal rivers, of which the Belize River is the most important. None is navigable by vessels over five feet draught and few for any notable distance. An estimated 92% of the mainland is under forest.
Climate

The climate is sub-tropical and on the whole agreeable to non-tropical peoples. In the capital, Belize City, the temperature range during the year is 50° to 96° with an average mean of 78.5°. Most of the year the heat and humidity are tempered by sea breezes. This description applies with small variation along the whole coastal area. Inland the day temperatures, during the dry season, tend to be higher, up to 100°, but drop considerably at night. Rainfall increases from north to south :

Stann Creek (upper south)147

There are two dry seasons, the main one from March to May and the other in August and September (called the Maugre Season).
The Colony has been struck from time to time by hurricanes.
In recent years:
a hurricane struck Belize City on the 10th September 1931 causing heavy loss of life and property;

on the 27th September 1955 Hurricane "Janet" struck the northern part of the country completely destroying the town of Corozal and damaging dozens of villages in the Orange Walk District. Damage was estimated at over $4 million.
on the 15th July 1960 Hurricane "Abby"

BRITISH HONDURAS
struck the area of the Sittee River, south of Stann Creek, causing some damage to crops and dwellings;

on the 24th July 1961 Hurricane "Anna" wrought fairly extensive damage to crops and houses in the villages of Seine Bight and Placencia and Sittee River and almost completely destroyed banana plantations at Waha Leaf in the Stann Creek District;
on the 31st October 1961 Hurricane "Hattie" caused 262 deaths and most serious damage to Belize City, Stann Creek and other parts of the country.
History

The present Settlement was apparently established in 1638 but British sea rovers were frequenting the Bay from long before that year and there is some evidence that a Settlement was formed in or around 1603. Logwood was the attraction, and the 1638 Settlement was augmented from time to time by sea rovers and—notably after the capture of Jamaica by British forces in 1655—by disbanded British soldiers and sailors. In the Treaty of Madrid, 1670, Spain recognized Britain's sovereignty over the Bay Settlement.

Concurrently another and larger British Settlement was building up on the Mosquito Indian Coast, far to the south of British Honduras. Modern historians frequently confuse the two Settlements; but although they assisted each other on occasion, they were entirely separate organizations with quite different forms of government. The Mosquito Indians stubbornly and successfully resisted Spanish intrusion but welcomed and became loyal allies of the British settlers. The Mosquito Coast settlers were known as the Shoremen and the Bay settlers as the Baymen. In 1786 Britain handed over the Mosquito Coast to Spain in return for a small and comparatively worthless strip of land contiguous to the Bay Settlement. Some of the Shoremen moved to Jamaica and the rest were compulsorily evacuated to the strip, where they became merged with the Baymen.

The Bay Settlement was a piece of purely British pioneering. The Spaniards found plentiful supplies of logwood, and Indians for slaves, in Northern Yucatan and settled there. Southern Yucatan, on the other hand, was by then practically devoid of Indians and its littoral was a wilderness of mosquito-infested mangrove swamps guarded by lee-shore reefs and tricky channels. The Spaniards therefore left this coast alone until the British began to settle around the mouth of the Belize River. The export of logwood from the Bay Settlement broke the highly profitable Spanish monopoly, brought great prosperity to Britain's wool and cloth-dyeing trades and caused Spain to launch attack after attack on the Settlement in an effort to drive out her competitors.

This armed aggression continued until 1798, when the Spaniards invaded the Settlement in strong naval and military force. The Baymen, although badly under-armed and heavily out-numbered, resisted and, after several days of skirmishing, the forces met in sea battle off St. George's Cay. Supported by H.M. Sloop Merlin , the Baymen fought with such determination that the Spaniards were forced to retreat. This was Spain's last attempt to dislodge the Baymen by force of arms.
Until 1786 the Baymen governed themselves. In that year, after many petitions from the Baymen, England appointed and sent out a

Superintendent, but the office was allowed to lapse in 1791. In 1797 Colonel Thomas Barrow was appointed Superintendent and was also given the title of Commander-in-Chief (held by the Governors of the Colony ever since) to enable him to organize the defence against the obviously impending Spanish attack. Thereafter the office of Superintendent continued until 1862, when it was replaced by that of Lieutenant-Governor, under the Governor of Jamaica and the Settlement raised to the status of a Colony. The Baymen resented being subordinated to Jamaica and in 1884 the Colony was detached from Jamaica and given a Governor.
Constitution

In 1954 the Legislative Council was replaced by a Legislative Assembly composed of nine elected members, three ex officio members and a Speaker and three unofficial members nominated by the Governor. The status of the Executive Council was changed to that of chief instrument of policy and it was composed of the Governor, as Chairman, three ex officio members and six members of the Legislative Assembly. The latter were elected by the Assembly but included not less than two of the nominated unofficial members of that body. Universal adult suffrage was also introduced in 1954, and the first general election under the new Constitution was held on 28th April 1954. The only fully organized party, the Peoples' United Party, gained eight of the nine elected seats. The principle of Steering Members, appointed by the Governor from among the unofficial members of the Executive Council, as the first step towards full ministerial status, was adopted in 1955.
At the general elections which took place on the 20th March 1957 the People's United Party gained all nine of the elected seats.

In February 1960 a Conference was convened in London by H.M. Government to consider inter alia proposals for the development of the Constitution of British Honduras. This conference was attended by the Secretary of State for the Colonies and the Minister of State with their Advisers; the Governor of British Honduras and official and unofficial members of the Government; and the leaders of the political parties in the territory.

The Conference decided to adopt a ministerial form of Government based on a revised Legislative Assembly and Executive Council. This revised Constitution was introduced in March 1961 following a general election. The new Legislative Assembly presided over by a Speaker, consisted of 18 members elected by the general public, five nominated members and two ex-officio members (the Chief Secretary (formerly known as the Colonial Secretary) and the Attorney General). The normal life of the Assembly was four years. The revised Executive Council under the chairmanship of the Governor, consisted of two ex-officio members (the Chief Secretary, responsible inter alia for defence, security and external affairs, and the Attorney General) and six unofficial members holding various departmental portfolios as Ministers, of whom at least one was a nominated member of the Legislative Assembly. The leader of the political party obtaining a majority at a general election for the Legislative Assembly seats was appointed First Minister and the remaining five unofficial members of the Executive Council were elected by the unofficial members of the Legislative Assembly from among their own number. As regards the five nominated

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

members of the Legislative Assembly, these were filled by the Governor, after consultation with the First Minister in respect of two seats and after consultation with the leader of the minority party in respect of one seat, the remaining two seats being filled by the Governor after consultation with the leaders of both the majority and minority parties. Further details are set out in a White Paper issued by H.M. Government as Cmnd. 984.

At the general election held in March 1961, the People's United Party, led by Mr. George Price, won all the 18 elected seats in the Legislative Assembly. Mr. Price was thereupon appointed First Minister and his Ministry has since held office.
The former post of Financial Secretary was replaced by that of Permanent Secretary, Ministry of Finance, a Ministry which is in the portfolio of the First Minister.

At a Constitutional Conference held in London in July 1963, it was agreed that the Colony should have a Ministerial system of internal self-government with a two-chamber legislature. Under the new Constitution which was introduced on the 6th January 1964, the Governor has special responsibilities for defence, external affairs, internal security and the safeguarding of the terms and conditions of service of public officers. Further, for so long as the Government of British Honduras continues to receive moneys from the United Kingdom Government in the form of grant-in-aid of the current revenues the Governor has a special responsibility for maintaining or securing the financial and economic stability of British Honduras and for ensuring that any condition attached to any financial grant or loan made by Her Majesty's Government is complied with.

The Executive Council has been replaced by a Cabinet consisting of a Premier and other Ministers. Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier. The Governor appoints as Premier the person who appears to him to be likely to command the support of the majority party in the House of Representatives.
The bi-cameral Legislature is known as the National Assembly and comprises a House of Representatives and a Senate.

The House of Representatives consists of 18 members elected under the system of universal adult suffrage. The Speaker may be elected by the House from amongst its own number or from outside the House but the Deputy Speaker is elected by the House from amongst its own number.

The Senate consists of eight members appointed by the Governor. Five are appointed on the advice of the Premier, two on the advice of the Leader of the Opposition and one after consulting such persons as the Governor considers appropriate. The President may be elected by the Senate from amongst its own number, or from outside the Senate, but the Vice-President is elected by the Senate from amongst its own number.
Land Policy

The alienation of Crown land is carefully controlled to prevent, as far as possible, the acquisition of such land for purely speculative, non-productive purposes. No racial discrimination is exercised.
During 1953 and 1954 a land use survey was carried out. A land policy, based on the findings and recommendations of the Survey Team, has been formulated and was published by Government in 1958.
Taxation

Income tax is levied on a graduated scale rising from 5% on the first $500 of chargeable income to 45% on all income over $25,000. Surtax is levied on the first $2,000 in excess of $8,000 of chargeable income at the rate of 10%; on the next $5,000 at the rate of 15%; on the next $5,000 at the rate of 20%; and on the remainder at 25%. Deductions allowed against income are a personal allowance of $600, plus allowances for wife, children, dependent relatives, life assurance premiums, earned income and gifts to charity. The rate of tax on companies is 40% of the chargeable income of the company.

The Public Debt of the Colony at the end of 1964 amounted to $7,328,704. Sinking funds on hand amounted to $1,422,653, leaving $5,906,051 as the net outstanding debt.
Financial Assistance towards Reconstruction after Hurricane "Hattie" (1961)
The Government of the United Kingdom made approximately £5 million available for hurricane rehabilitation and reconstruction after Hurricane "Hattie" in 1961.
Of this amount some £1 million was spent on emergency assistance immediately following the hurricane.
Of the balance, £1½ million was earmarked for the establishment of a new capital city some 50 miles inland. This project has now been approved and some preliminary clearing has already been done.

The remaining balance was approved for the hurricane reconstruction and development programme which ends on 30th June 1966. The major item in this programme comprises housing grants and loans, most of which have been issued.
Currency

The unit of currency is the British Honduras dollar which is exchanged at four to the £ sterling. Notes of $20, $10, $5, $2 and $1 are issued and there are coins of 50, 25 and 10 cents pieces in cupro nickel, 5 cents in nickel/brass and bronze 1 cent pieces.
Development Plans

In addition to almost $25 million contributed from Colonial Development and Welfare funds during the period 1945–1965, a further allocation of $4·8 million was made to this country under the Commonwealth Development Act, 1963, for the period up to 31st March 1966. The major portion of the funds is being used to make a start with the

It envisages a total expenditure of about $48 million; of this sum it is now proposed that about $14 million should be financed by loan, a small part of which is to be raised locally. A Development Finance Corporation has been established under the provisions of the Development Corporation Ordinance No. 2 of 1961 as amended by Ordinance No. 15 of 1963. The purpose of the Corporation is to expand and strengthen the economy of the country. The share capital of the Corporation is $4 million divided into 400,000 shares at $10 a share. There are just over 2,600 shareholders holding 31,885 shares.

The Reconstruction and Development Corporation, which was set up to manage certain funds allocated to the general reconstruction programme and to secure the laying out of and the development of new towns, was again active in 1964. It approved grants and loans for housing rehabilitation to the value of over $2\frac{1}{2}$ million up to the end of 1964. Loan funds amounting to approximately $40,000 were also provided for citrus and cocoa growers, fishermen and tradesmen during 1963.
Other Development

Tate and Lyle Ltd. has taken over the Corozal Sugar Factory Ltd. and its subsidiaries. The Corozal Sugar Factory Ltd. produced just over 35,000 tons of sugar in 1965 for the domestic and export market. Tate and Lyle Ltd. intends to expand the existing operations of the present factory to 45,000 tons. Another factory in the Orange Walk district to produce a further 30,000 tons of sugar by 1967 is now under construction. The total estimated investment is $31 million. It is expected that the two factories will together achieve a total production of 175,000 tons or more by 1971/72 subject to market conditions.

United Fruit Co. Ltd., the parent Company of locally incorporated Belize Banana Company, signed an agreement with Government to investigate the establishment of operations in the Swasey-Bladen-Monkey River Area which would have involved the growing and processing of bananas and other agricultural crops, and the by-products from such crops, for sale in world markets. As a result of detailed engineering studies completed in accordance with the terms of the agreement the company decided that the project was not economically feasible in the light of present and projected conditions.

Hercules B. H. Ltd., and its subsidiary Pine Harvesters Ltd., which together invested some $5 million in a project to produce resin from pine stumps, informed Government during 1965 that they were reluctantly compelled to eventually cease operations due to (a) changes in the world supply of resin, (b) unforseen difficulties in harvesting pine stumps, (c) information as a result of
further recent surveys that the quantity of available pine stumps is much less than previously estimated.

Salada Foods Inc. acquired the Commonwealth Development Corporation holdings in the Stann Creek Valley and planted out over 1,000,000 acres of citrus. It has established a plant for the production of frozen concentrates for the Canadian market. The Citrus orchards in the Stann creek Valley recovered quickly from the severe damage done by Hurricane "Hattie".
Education

Primary education is compulsory between the ages of 6 and 14. It is conducted almost entirely by the denominations, with a government subsidy. Similarly, with the exception of a Government Technical College, the secondary schools are all run by the denominations.

The Minister of Education is responsible for education under the general authority of the Education Ordinance, 1962. His Chief Executive Officer is the Principal Secretary in the Ministry of Education, and his Chief Administrative Officer is the Chief Education Officer. A Primary Education Board and an Advisory Council for Secondary and Further Education advise him on the respective branches of education.

In 1964 there were 133 primary grant-aided schools (of which 2 were maintained by Government), 24 private primary schools, 15 recognised denominational secondary schools and a Technical College run by the Government, as well as a special school for handicapped children conducted on a semi-private basis. The total enrolment in primary schools was 26,322, and in secondary schools 2,113. There were two Teacher Training Colleges offering a two-year course, and an Intermediate Teacher Training College giving an intensive twenty-week course for new entrants to the teaching profession.

There is no local institution at university level, and scholarships are awarded to the University of the West Indies and universities and similar institutions in Britain and other countries. The University maintains an Extra Mural Department with a Resident Tutor in Belize City.

Expenditure on education from the local budget in 1964 was $1,260,377, representing about 15% of the budget. In addition, Colonial Development and Welfare grants from the United Kingdom amounted to $732,533, covering the period April 1964 to March 1966.
Libraries, etc.

A statutory Library Board conducts the British Honduras Library Service, which falls under the portfolio of the Minister of Education and Housing. The Library Service aims at complete coverage of the country, and at the 31st December 1964 had 63 service-points—a Central Library, a Children's Library, a Technical Library, a branch and two smaller points in Belize City, a branch in each of the five district towns, and 52 sub-libraries in villages throughout the country. The majority of the service-points have reading and reference sections. The service is a free one. At the end of 1964 there were 15,907 registered members, and the bookstock was 64,435 volumes.

The Central Library is accommodated in the Baron Bliss Institute, which also houses the Extra Mural Department of the University of the West Indies, and has a modern stage and auditorium. Evening classes are conducted regularly by the Extra Mural Department, and dramatic productions are held. The Library Service held a successful Book Fair during 1964.

The British Council has an office and a Representative in Belize City and provides a small library for the public.
Health

The country is relatively free from endemic diseases. Malaria, which was most prevalent, is no longer a problem, owing to a Malaria Eradication Programme operated on a tripartite basis by Government, W.H.O., and U.N.I.C.E.F. The main general hospital, with 162 beds, is in Belize City and there are small hospitals in each of the 5 districts, all run by Government, and with a total bed strength of 142. There is also a 13 bed private hospital in the capital. Some 20 Government and Mission rural dispensaries in the more important villages are each in charge of a qualified rural health nurse. At present there are four villages without nurses due to a shortage of staff. A nurses training school and hostel was opened in new quarters in December 1963.

Two Health Centres, a Venereal Disease Clinic, an infirmary and a mental hospital are maintained by Government in Belize City. A temporary Tuberculosis Sanitorium, also maintained by Government, has been established some 18 miles outside Belize City to replace the one destroyed in the 1961 hurricane.
Expenditure on medical and health services from general revenue during 1964 was $941,415.
Communications

The Belize International Airport is located about ten miles north-west of Belize City. It is the principal airport of the Colony. International air services to the country are operated by the following airlines:
Transportes Aereos Centro Americanos, S.A. (T.A.C.A. International Airlines). Servicio Aereo de Honduras, S.A. (S.A.H.S.A.). Transportes Aereos Nacionales, S.A. (T.A.N. airlines). Maya Airlines.

Thrice weekly services to New Orleans (U.S.A.), Belize City and San Salvador via Guatemala City is operated by T.A.C.A. The Miami/Belize City/Tegucigalpa and San Pedro Sula (Honduras) route is operated four times weekly by T.A.N. S.A.H.S.A. operate a twice weekly service from and to San Pedro Sula. Maya Airways operate an internal scheduled air service to district towns and other parts of the country. Air Taxi Service, a new operator, is providing non-scheduled services throughout the country.
There are 605 miles of all-weather main feeder roads, 177 miles of cart roads and bush trails and 158 miles of dry-weather roads maintained by Government. Broken down further, Government maintain:
183 miles of asphalt road, 270 miles of gravel road, 268 miles of marl road, and 271 miles of dirt road.

The United Fruit Company maintains a weekly passenger and cargo service between Belize City and New Orleans, U.S.A. The T. and J. Harrison and the Royal Netherlands S/S. lines offer a regular fortnightly cargo service between Belize City and the United Kingdom and Europe respectively, the Royal Netherlands ship offering limited passenger accommodation. The "K" line is now operating a monthly cargo service between Belize City and Japan. The Kirkconnell Bros., Foster

Enterprise Company and the Howthorne Trader Company maintain an irregular freighter and passenger service between Jamaica, Cayman Islands and Belize City. The Esso Tanker operates a monthly service from Aruba with petroleum products. A three times weekly cargo service is now established by the Canada, Jamaica Line from Canada to Belize City.
In 1964, a total of 43 steam and motor vessels and 27 sailing vessels, 1,806 gross tons in all, registered at the port of Belize City.
Telecommunications

Cable & Wireless Ltd. have been granted a licence to operate the external telecommunications service of the country which also includes radio-telephone facilities with major towns and cities in the U.S.A., the Caribbean and the United Kingdom.
Cable & Wireless Ltd., on behalf of Government, operate a fully automatic telephone system within Belize City.
There is also an internal telecommunications system in operation.
Information Service

On 1st November 1962, Information and Broadcasting were separated and two new Departments formed. The Government Information Service is now in charge of all Government publicity material, press liaison, campaigns and exhibitions to project the activities and policies of the Government to the people at home and abroad.
Broadcasting

Government operates a radio broadcasting service (Radio Belize) transmitting from Belize City on the following frequencies and powers: 840 kc/s. (361 metres), 20kW. and 3.3 Mc/s. (90 metres), 5 kW. to 20 kW. Programmes are given in English and Spanish and include both local and foreign news, announcements of local interest, local features, transcribed music and plays. Daily time totals about eleven hours and the Station began broadcasting commercial advertisements on payment of a fee as from the 1st July, 1964.
Principal Occupations
Most of the working population are engaged in agriculture on their own account.

BRITISH HONDURAS
Main Crops and Products

Indian corn (maize), beans and peas, rice, sugar, cassava and yams are the principal food crops of the country. The main export crops are citrus fruits, processed citrus and sugar. Cattle, pigs and poultry are raised throughout the country. Sugar is the principal export product in the form of raw sugar and molasses. Timber is also a main export product. These consist mainly of mahogany, pine and cedar. Rosewood, Banak, Santa Maria and other woods are also exported. Efforts are being directed to building up the country's scale-fish industry, and an export trade to the United States, chiefly in lobsters, is well established.

Industries established in and near Belize City are a sawmill, a cigarette factory, two aluminium door, window and louvre factories, a garment factory and several small furniture factories. In other parts of the country there are two canneries operating in the Stann Creek Valley on citrus products, a cement block and tile factory at the border of the Belize-Cayo Districts, a resin factory in Stann Creek District and a sugar factory in the Corozal District.
